attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- spec-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level SPEC.md service specification — the language-agnostic behavioral spec that sits at a project's root and describes what a long-running service or automation does (problem statement, goals/non-goals, components, domain model). Applies when the user says things like "write a spec for X", "draft a specification", "update SPEC.md to add Y", "revise the non-goals", "add a new component to the spec", or hands over notes and asks for them to be turned into a formal service spec. Also applies when the user references an existing SPEC.md and wants edits, even if they don't explicitly use the word "spec". Covers both greenfield creation and targeted updates of an existing file. Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
- lightfast-aeo: Use when authoring or reviewing any Lightfast content destined for AI engine retrieval —
release notes, blog posts, docs pages, integration pages, anything published to the
public site. Triggers on requests to write, draft, rewrite, or audit prose that should
be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews, or other answer engines,
even when the user doesn't say "AEO", "citations", or "answer engine". Per-content-type
skills compose this for voice and section ordering — load this one too whenever they
activate.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-debug: General-purpose Lightfast debugging workflow. Triggers when the user asks to debug,
investigate, trace, reproduce, inspect, or diagnose behavior across the app, platform,
Inngest workflows, database state, connected providers, or observability tooling. Use as
the broad entrypoint when no narrower skill is clearly better, when the failing surface is
unclear, or when a bug may cross multiple systems. Prefer `lightfast-db`,
`lightfast-inngest`, or `lightfast-sdk` for direct single-surface requests that clearly map
to one of those tools.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

Use when user: configures tasks/workflows/pipelines, creates packages, sets up
monorepo, shares code between apps, runs changed/affected packages, debugs cache,
or has apps/packages directories.
- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX
- research_codebase: Document codebase as-is with thoughts directory for historical context
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Lightfast Specification
2	
3	Status: Draft v0 (high-level, language-agnostic)
4	
5	Last Updated: 2026-04-15
6	
7	Purpose: Define the operating infrastructure between agents and apps — the layer through which agents and teams observe, reason, and act across a product-development tool stack.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Mission
12	
13	Be the operating layer for every team — so agents and people can observe, reason, and act across the entire tool stack through a single system.
14	
15	## Vision
16	
17	Any agent or engineer can understand what is happening, why it happened, and what should happen next — across every tool, every team, every decision — without knowing which tools exist or how they work.
18	
19	---
20	
21	## 1. Problem Statement
22	
23	Lightfast is an operating layer that sits between agents and apps. The layer ingests events from connected tools, stores them as a cited temporal graph, resolves intent into action against the correct tool, and enforces team-defined invariants continuously.
24	
25	The layer addresses four operational problems:
26	
27	- Tool memory is siloed. Events live only in the tool that produced them, so no system correlates causality, ownership, or relationships across the stack.
28	- Agents target tools directly. Every new agent means N×M integrations, drifting API usage, and no shared context across runs.
29	- Team rules are enforced by convention. Release checks, review gates, and policy rules live in documents, not in a kernel that applies them at all times.
30	- Answers cannot cite their source. Summaries without evidence cannot be trusted when humans or agents act on them.
31	
32	Important boundary:
33	
34	- Lightfast is an operating layer. It mediates between tools; it does not replace them.
35	- Lightfast is not a data warehouse, a BI tool, or a general-purpose observability platform.
36	- A successful operation ends at a cited answer or a resolved action against a connected tool, not at raw data delivery.
37	
38	## 2. Goals and Non-Goals
39	
40	### 2.1 Goals
41	
42	- Observe every event across every connected tool automatically and continuously.
43	- Store events as immutable, causally ordered facts with semantic embeddings in a temporal graph.
44	- Cite every answer, action, and decision back to source events.
45	- Resolve agent intent into action against the correct connected tool without per-agent integration work.
46	- Enforce team-defined invariants continuously, not on a schedule.
47	- Expose the same primitives to agents and to people (REST API, SDK, MCP, webhooks).
48	- Preserve complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for model training.
49	
50	### 2.2 Non-Goals
51	
52	- Replacing existing tools in the stack. Teams keep using what they use.
53	- General-purpose analytics, business intelligence, or data warehousing.
54	- Black-box decisions. Every action traces to source events; no summary ships without verification.
55	- Rebuilding a unified UI for every connected tool.
56	- Agent orchestration as a product surface. Agents operate through Lightfast; Lightfast does not orchestrate agents.
57	
58	## 3. System Overview
59	
60	### 3.1 Abstraction Layers
61	
62	Lightfast is a pipeline: **Observe → Remember → Reason → Act.** Each layer is a distinct concern.
63	
64	1. `Observe`
65	   - Ingests events from connected tools via webhooks, polling, or push APIs.
66	   - Normalizes platform payloads into a stable event model.
67	
68	2. `Remember`
69	   - Stores events as immutable facts with causal ordering.
70	   - Resolves entities across tools (commits, issues, deployments, incidents, people).
71	   - Maintains a temporal graph and semantic embeddings over those entities.
72	
73	3. `Reason`
74	   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
75	   - Detects patterns, predicts outcomes, and enforces invariants.
76	   - Produces cited answers to queries from agents and people.
77	
78	4. `Act`
79	   - Resolves intent into write operations against the correct connected tool.
80	   - Performs side effects (PR comments, issue updates, messages, deploys) with provenance attached.
81	
82	### 3.2 Main Components
83	
84	1. `Connection Layer`
85	   - Manages OAuth flows, credential storage, webhook registration, and backfills per tool.
86	   - Further specification deferred.
87	
88	2. `Event Store`
89	   - Append-only store of normalized events with causal ordering.
90	   - Further specification deferred.
91	
92	3. `Graph and Embeddings`
93	   - Temporal graph of entities and relationships with semantic embeddings.
94	   - Further specification deferred.
95	
96	4. `Reasoning Runtime`
97	   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
98	   - Further specification deferred.
99	
100	5. `Action Resolver`
101	   - Translates intent into write operations against connected tools.
102	   - Further specification deferred.
103	
104	6. `Access Surfaces`
105	   - REST API, TypeScript SDK, MCP server, webhooks.
106	   - Further specification deferred.
107	
108	### 3.3 External Dependencies
109	
110	- Connected tools (for example GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel). The set is extensible.
111	- Identity provider for tenant authentication.
112	- Vector index and graph storage.
113	- Background job runtime for ingestion and long-running workflows.
114	
115	## 4. Core Domain Model
116	
117	Entity field specifications are deferred to a future revision. This section lists the primary entity types and their present state.
118	
119	Status tags:
120	- `(shipped v0.1.0)` — entity exists in the running system and is covered by the public API.
121	- `(partial v0.1.0)` — entity exists in limited form; the role defined here is broader than what ships.
122	- `(planned)` — entity is part of the designed system but has no implementation yet.
123	
124	### 4.1 Entities
125	
126	#### 4.1.1 Event `(shipped v0.1.0)`
127	
128	An immutable fact emitted by a connected tool and normalized into the Lightfast event model. Once recorded, an event is never modified.
129	
130	#### 4.1.2 Entity `(shipped v0.1.0)`
131	
132	A resolved object in the temporal graph — for example a commit, issue, deployment, incident, or person — synthesized from one or more events.
133	
134	#### 4.1.3 Connection `(shipped v0.1.0)`
135	
136	A binding between a tenant and a connected tool, including credentials, scopes, and lifecycle state.
137	
138	#### 4.1.4 Skill `(planned)`
139	
140	A composable capability that reads memory and optionally invokes the Action Resolver.
141	
142	#### 4.1.5 Workflow `(planned)`
143	
144	An ordered composition of skills, triggered by events or schedules.
145	
146	#### 4.1.6 Rule `(planned)`
147	
148	A team-defined invariant continuously enforced against memory.
149	
150	#### 4.1.7 Agent `(partial v0.1.0)`
151	
152	A principal — human or automated — that interacts with Lightfast through an access surface. In `v0.1.0` an agent is any caller authenticated by an API key or session token; the expanded role (identity, permissions scoped per skill and rule) is planned.
153	
154	## 5. Principles
155	
156	- **Primitives over features.** Every capability is a configuration of the same building blocks, not a new subsystem.
157	- **Events are facts.** Immutable, causally ordered. Interpretations layer on top. History is never rewritten.
158	- **Intent over API calls.** Agents express what they want. The system resolves how.
159	- **Cite everything.** Every answer, every action, every decision traces back to source events.
160	- **Privacy by default.** Complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for training.
161	
162	## 6. Measures
163	
164	- **Coverage.** Fraction of a team's tool stack observed and connected.
165	- **Precision.** Accuracy of cited answers against ground-truth source events.
166	- **Latency.** Time from event ingestion to availability in memory, and from query to cited answer.
167	- **Trust.** Fraction of answers and actions with verifiable source evidence and causal rationale.
168	- **Adoption.** Time-to-integrate per tool and density of agent activity routed through the layer.
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Deferred Sections
173	
174	The following sections are intentionally omitted at this draft level and will be populated in future revisions:
175	
176	- Detailed field definitions for each entity in Section 4.
177	- Event lifecycle and state machines.
178	- Consistency, ordering, and idempotency guarantees.
179	- Authorization model and scope primitives.
180	- Error classes and recovery behavior.
181	- Observability contract (logs, metrics, traces).
182	- Conformance and compatibility rules.
183

file.content:
# Lightfast Specification

Status: Draft v0 (high-level, language-agnostic)

Last Updated: 2026-04-15

Purpose: Define the operating infrastructure between agents and apps — the layer through which agents and teams observe, reason, and act across a product-development tool stack.

---

## Mission

Be the operating layer for every team — so agents and people can observe, reason, and act across the entire tool stack through a single system.

## Vision

Any agent or engineer can understand what is happening, why it happened, and what should happen next — across every tool, every team, every decision — without knowing which tools exist or how they work.

---

## 1. Problem Statement

Lightfast is an operating layer that sits between agents and apps. The layer ingests events from connected tools, stores them as a cited temporal graph, resolves intent into action against the correct tool, and enforces team-defined invariants continuously.

The layer addresses four operational problems:

- Tool memory is siloed. Events live only in the tool that produced them, so no system correlates causality, ownership, or relationships across the stack.
- Agents target tools directly. Every new agent means N×M integrations, drifting API usage, and no shared context across runs.
- Team rules are enforced by convention. Release checks, review gates, and policy rules live in documents, not in a kernel that applies them at all times.
- Answers cannot cite their source. Summaries without evidence cannot be trusted when humans or agents act on them.

Important boundary:

- Lightfast is an operating layer. It mediates between tools; it does not replace them.
- Lightfast is not a data warehouse, a BI tool, or a general-purpose observability platform.
- A successful operation ends at a cited answer or a resolved action against a connected tool, not at raw data delivery.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

### 2.1 Goals

- Observe every event across every connected tool automatically and continuously.
- Store events as immutable, causally ordered facts with semantic embeddings in a temporal graph.
- Cite every answer, action, and decision back to source events.
- Resolve agent intent into action against the correct connected tool without per-agent integration work.
- Enforce team-defined invariants continuously, not on a schedule.
- Expose the same primitives to agents and to people (REST API, SDK, MCP, webhooks).
- Preserve complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for model training.

### 2.2 Non-Goals

- Replacing existing tools in the stack. Teams keep using what they use.
- General-purpose analytics, business intelligence, or data warehousing.
- Black-box decisions. Every action traces to source events; no summary ships without verification.
- Rebuilding a unified UI for every connected tool.
- Agent orchestration as a product surface. Agents operate through Lightfast; Lightfast does not orchestrate agents.

## 3. System Overview

### 3.1 Abstraction Layers

Lightfast is a pipeline: **Observe → Remember → Reason → Act.** Each layer is a distinct concern.

1. `Observe`
   - Ingests events from connected tools via webhooks, polling, or push APIs.
   - Normalizes platform payloads into a stable event model.

2. `Remember`
   - Stores events as immutable facts with causal ordering.
   - Resolves entities across tools (commits, issues, deployments, incidents, people).
   - Maintains a temporal graph and semantic embeddings over those entities.

3. `Reason`
   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
   - Detects patterns, predicts outcomes, and enforces invariants.
   - Produces cited answers to queries from agents and people.

4. `Act`
   - Resolves intent into write operations against the correct connected tool.
   - Performs side effects (PR comments, issue updates, messages, deploys) with provenance attached.

### 3.2 Main Components

1. `Connection Layer`
   - Manages OAuth flows, credential storage, webhook registration, and backfills per tool.
   - Further specification deferred.

2. `Event Store`
   - Append-only store of normalized events with causal ordering.
   - Further specification deferred.

3. `Graph and Embeddings`
   - Temporal graph of entities and relationships with semantic embeddings.
   - Further specification deferred.

4. `Reasoning Runtime`
   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
   - Further specification deferred.

5. `Action Resolver`
   - Translates intent into write operations against connected tools.
   - Further specification deferred.

6. `Access Surfaces`
   - REST API, TypeScript SDK, MCP server, webhooks.
   - Further specification deferred.

### 3.3 External Dependencies

- Connected tools (for example GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel). The set is extensible.
- Identity provider for tenant authentication.
- Vector index and graph storage.
- Background job runtime for ingestion and long-running workflows.

## 4. Core Domain Model

Entity field specifications are deferred to a future revision. This section lists the primary entity types and their present state.

Status tags:
- `(shipped v0.1.0)` — entity exists in the running system and is covered by the public API.
- `(partial v0.1.0)` — entity exists in limited form; the role defined here is broader than what ships.
- `(planned)` — entity is part of the designed system but has no implementation yet.

### 4.1 Entities

#### 4.1.1 Event `(shipped v0.1.0)`

An immutable fact emitted by a connected tool and normalized into the Lightfast event model. Once recorded, an event is never modified.

#### 4.1.2 Entity `(shipped v0.1.0)`

A resolved object in the temporal graph — for example a commit, issue, deployment, incident, or person — synthesized from one or more events.

#### 4.1.3 Connection `(shipped v0.1.0)`

A binding between a tenant and a connected tool, including credentials, scopes, and lifecycle state.

#### 4.1.4 Skill `(planned)`

A composable capability that reads memory and optionally invokes the Action Resolver.

#### 4.1.5 Workflow `(planned)`

An ordered composition of skills, triggered by events or schedules.

#### 4.1.6 Rule `(planned)`

A team-defined invariant continuously enforced against memory.

#### 4.1.7 Agent `(partial v0.1.0)`

A principal — human or automated — that interacts with Lightfast through an access surface. In `v0.1.0` an agent is any caller authenticated by an API key or session token; the expanded role (identity, permissions scoped per skill and rule) is planned.

## 5. Principles

- **Primitives over features.** Every capability is a configuration of the same building blocks, not a new subsystem.
- **Events are facts.** Immutable, causally ordered. Interpretations layer on top. History is never rewritten.
- **Intent over API calls.** Agents express what they want. The system resolves how.
- **Cite everything.** Every answer, every action, every decision traces back to source events.
- **Privacy by default.** Complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for training.

## 6. Measures

- **Coverage.** Fraction of a team's tool stack observed and connected.
- **Precision.** Accuracy of cited answers against ground-truth source events.
- **Latency.** Time from event ingestion to availability in memory, and from query to cited answer.
- **Trust.** Fraction of answers and actions with verifiable source evidence and causal rationale.
- **Adoption.** Time-to-integrate per tool and density of agent activity routed through the layer.

---

## Deferred Sections

The following sections are intentionally omitted at this draft level and will be populated in future revisions:

- Detailed field definitions for each entity in Section 4.
- Event lifecycle and state machines.
- Consistency, ordering, and idempotency guarantees.
- Authorization model and scope primitives.
- Error classes and recovery behavior.
- Observability contract (logs, metrics, traces).
- Conformance and compatibility rules.

content.text:
I've read the template and your SPEC.md. Two things worth flagging before I start:

**The template is Australian** (ABN/ACN, GST, Australian laws, AUD) — is Lightfast incorporated in Australia? That determines whether to keep those fields or swap for the right jurisdiction.

**Editing .docx directly is awkward** in this env (no pandoc). My recommendation: copy the extracted template into `thoughts/shared/business-plan.md` as a working markdown mirror that I can iterate on with you, then convert back to .docx at the end. The tradeoff is you lose the live Word form controls (dropdowns, date pickers) while drafting, but we gain fast turns and git history.

Alternatively I can keep the original .docx at `thoughts/shared/business-plan.docx` as the source of truth and hand you a change-list to paste in each round — slower but preserves the template formatting.

Which do you want? And can you confirm the jurisdiction + tell me which sections to prioritize first (I'd suggest the narrative sections — Why/Vision/Mission/Problem/Solution/Target Market/Competition/SWOT — since those derive cleanly from SPEC.md; finance + registration details need your input)?

message.content: makes sense go with markdown approach.

1	---
2	name: Lightfast positioning — operating system for startups
3	description: Lightfast's current product thesis (Apr 2026): an OS that runs the startup-building lifecycle for 1-30 person teams, not a memory layer
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: 0091b36e-3d7e-49e4-bbf3-e9906334732b
6	---
7	Lightfast is positioned as an **operating system that runs the entire startup-building lifecycle** for 1–30 person teams. It orchestrates pipelines across ops, GTM, dev, design, support, feedback, and errors. Unifies company knowledge into expressible logic reusable anywhere. Ambient intelligence puts startup-building on autopilot.
8	
9	Why-now thesis: **code is commoditized** (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex have flattened the cost of shipping). The remaining hard problem is everything *around* code — knowing what to build next, customer feedback triage, GTM motions, support, ops. If code is solved, the new bottleneck is the orchestration of the company itself. Lightfast commoditizes building a startup the way Claude Code commoditized writing one.
10	
11	**Why:** Prior framings — "memory layer for teams," "search API with citations," the apps/www/src/config/pitch-deck-data.ts copy — are stale and undersell the scope. Talking about Lightfast as a memory product misrepresents the current vision.
12	
13	**How to apply:** Frame Lightfast as a startup OS, not a memory layer or search product. The memory layer is one process the OS runs, not the headline. When writing pitch/marketing/docs copy, lead with the OS framing and "ambient intelligence → autopilot" outcome.
14

file.content:
---
name: Lightfast positioning — operating system for startups
description: Lightfast's current product thesis (Apr 2026): an OS that runs the startup-building lifecycle for 1-30 person teams, not a memory layer
type: project
originSessionId: 0091b36e-3d7e-49e4-bbf3-e9906334732b
---
Lightfast is positioned as an **operating system that runs the entire startup-building lifecycle** for 1–30 person teams. It orchestrates pipelines across ops, GTM, dev, design, support, feedback, and errors. Unifies company knowledge into expressible logic reusable anywhere. Ambient intelligence puts startup-building on autopilot.

Why-now thesis: **code is commoditized** (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex have flattened the cost of shipping). The remaining hard problem is everything *around* code — knowing what to build next, customer feedback triage, GTM motions, support, ops. If code is solved, the new bottleneck is the orchestration of the company itself. Lightfast commoditizes building a startup the way Claude Code commoditized writing one.

**Why:** Prior framings — "memory layer for teams," "search API with citations," the apps/www/src/config/pitch-deck-data.ts copy — are stale and undersell the scope. Talking about Lightfast as a memory product misrepresents the current vision.

**How to apply:** Frame Lightfast as a startup OS, not a memory layer or search product. The memory layer is one process the OS runs, not the headline. When writing pitch/marketing/docs copy, lead with the OS framing and "ambient intelligence → autopilot" outcome.

content.content:
1	# Lightfast Pitch Deck — Working Draft (2026-04)
2	
3	> Working content for the seed pitch deck being designed in Paper ("Daring ember" file).
4	> Format: 10 slides, 1440×900, dark editorial system, single red moment on slide 1.
5	> Voice: Linear cadence — short declaratives, active mechanical verbs, no hype words.
6	> Recurring nouns: Company → Program · Validation · Growth · Scale
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Slide 01 — Title `[LOCKED]`
11	
12	- **H1**: The operating system for startups.
13	- **Sub**: Accelerating the path from validation to scale.
14	- **Chrome**: `LIGHTFAST` · `PITCH · APRIL 2026` · `SEED ROUND` · `01 / 10 — TITLE`
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Slide 02 — Problem
19	
20	- **H1**: Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company.

# Lightfast — Business Plan

> Working markdown mirror of the business.gov.au business plan template.
> Source template: `Business plan template doc (1).docx` (April 2026 download).
> Voice rules: Linear cadence. Short declaratives. No hype words. Capitalize proprietary nouns (Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities).
> Status: **Draft 0** — narrative sections derived from `SPEC.md` and `thoughts/shared/pitch-deck-2026-04.md`. Data-dependent fields marked `[TBD — needs user input]`.

---

## Fields that need user input

Checklist of fields I can't derive from the codebase or memory. Fill these in and I'll fold them into the next revision.

- [ ] ABN / ACN and registered business name
- [ ] Date registered + state/territory
- [ ] Business structure (Pty Ltd? Sole trader? Company limited by shares?)
- [ ] Licences and permits (if any)
- [ ] Registered business address
- [ ] Phone / mobile
- [ ] Online profiles beyond website (LinkedIn, X, GitHub org)
- [ ] Equipment and major assets (laptop, dev hardware, servers if any)
- [ ] Registered IP (trade marks for "Lightfast"? logo?)
- [ ] Existing staff + advisors (names, roles, skills)
- [ ] Sources of advice and support (accountant, mentor, investors)
- [ ] Insurance in place (or needed): PI, public liability, cyber
- [ ] Finance — amount sought, prior funding history, current cash position
- [ ] Balance / P&L / cash flow forecasts (3 year)
- [ ] Succession plan
- [ ] Risk register (I'll draft, you review)

---

## Key details

### Registration details

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Business name | [TBD — Lightfast Pty Ltd? confirm registered name] |
| Date registered | [TBD] |
| State registered in | [TBD — Victoria? confirm] |
| Business structure | [TBD — confirm] |
| ABN | [TBD] |
| ACN | [TBD — if a company] |
| Licences and permits | None required for SaaS at current scope. Review before handling regulated data (health, finance). |

### Contact details

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | Jeevan Pillay |
| Phone | [TBD] |
| Mobile | [TBD] |
| Email | jp@lightfast.ai |
| Address | [TBD — Melbourne, VIC] |

### Online and social media details

| Type | Address / handle |
|---|---|
| Website | https://lightfast.ai |
| GitHub | https://github.com/lightfastai |
| LinkedIn | [TBD] |
| X / Twitter | [TBD] |

---

## The business

### Plan summary

> Complete this section last — summarise from the rest of the plan.

**What our business does.** Lightfast is an operating system for startups. It runs the work between tools — Programs, Processes, Memory, and Opportunities — so small teams can ship product, launch, support customers, and close the loop without stitching context by hand.

**Our target market.** Teams of 1 to 30 building software today. Solo founders, seed-stage teams, and Series A teams shipping daily on a fragmented tool stack.

**Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them and routes the work.

**Our business goals.**
- Close seed round ([TBD — $500K SAFE target from pitch deck]).
- Ship v1 runtime — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities — to design-partner teams.
- Reach [TBD — MRR target] by end of Year 1.

**Our history.** 12 months of development. 3,930 commits. $0 external funding to date. Prior experience across AI infrastructure, computational design, and fabrication systems. 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated context loss as a recurring top-pain point.

### Our why

Founders spend their day stitching context between tools. Code is cheaper than ever to write, but the work that surrounds it — deciding what to build next, triaging feedback, closing the loop on support, running GTM, catching errors — is still manual. Lightfast exists because coordination is the next bottleneck, and no one is building the layer that runs it.

### Our vision

Every startup runs on Lightfast. The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the company moving — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops — with the same compounding leverage that coding agents gave engineers.

### Our mission

Give small teams one system to run the company. Unify company knowledge into expressible logic that is reusable anywhere. Put the startup-building lifecycle on autopilot so founders spend time on judgment, not follow-up.

### Operations

#### Products and services

| Product / service | Description | Unit price (incl. GST) |
|---|---|---|
| Solo | 1 user · 3 Pipelines · Core Memory | Free |
| Team | Up to 30 users · all seven Pipelines · agent + MCP access | $50 / team / month [TBD — confirm per-team vs. per-user; pricing still open in pitch deck] |
| Scale | 30+ users · SSO · audit log · dedicated infra | Custom |

#### Production

---

## The market

### The problem

Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company. Coding agents flattened the cost of shipping. What remains is everything around code — priorities, support, launches, bugs, follow-up — and it still lives across disconnected tools. Founders and small teams spend their time stitching context together and keeping work from slipping. The product is faster to build. The company is still manual.

### Our solution

Lightfast gives startups one system to run the company — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops. Four primitives, built once, composable forever:

- **Programs** — plain-language operating spec for the company.
- **Processes** — managed workflows with clear state.
- **Memory** — shared context across the work, with citations back to source events.
- **Opportunities** — the system surfaces the highest-leverage next action across product, growth, support, and ops.

The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the work moving.

### Our target market

Teams of 1 to 30 shipping software today.

- **Solo founders** — building a product alone, context lives in their head and browser tabs.
- **2–10 person seed teams** — first hires made, tools multiplying, coordination cost rising.
- **10–30 person Series A teams** — every function represented, but glue-work absorbs the founder.

**How many.**
- 80K+ YC applications annually.
- ~50K US seed-stage companies funded annually.
- Millions of solo builders globally.

**Why underserved.** Existing tooling is built for one function at a time. Small teams carry the overlap themselves.

### Advertising and promotion

| Channel | Used for | Details |
|---|---|---|
| Content / AEO | Inbound via answer engines and search | Changelog, blog, docs structured for citation by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews. Lightfast's internal `lightfast-aeo` skill governs voice and structure. |
| Founder-led outbound | Seed round + design partners | Direct conversations with seed / Series A founders in network. |
| X / social | Build-in-public loop | Ship cadence visible; attract engineers and founders. |
| Partner ecosystem | Distribution via connected tools | GitHub Marketplace, Linear integrations directory, etc. |
| Community / events | [TBD — YC, local Melbourne startup community, founder dinners] | |

### The competition

Top categories we compete against.

#### Competitor 1 — Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex)

- **Strengths.** Powerful general agents. Deep Anthropic / OpenAI distribution. Flatten the cost of writing software.
- **Weaknesses.** General-purpose. Execute tasks; do not run a company's operating loops. No native Programs, Processes, or Opportunities surface.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast is the layer above: it decides what the company should do next, then routes work — including to these agents where appropriate. We compose with them, not against them on code generation.

#### Competitor 2 — Point solutions (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog)

- **Strengths.** Deep ownership of one pipeline each (issues, docs, support, analytics). Loved by users within their domain.
- **Weaknesses.** Each owns one pipeline. The work between them falls on the founder. None coordinate across product, GTM, support, and ops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast doesn't replace them. It observes them, remembers across them, and acts through them — with citations back to the source events.

#### Competitor 3 — Status quo (founder + tabs + context switching)

- **Strengths.** Zero vendor cost. Fully flexible. Founder knows everything in their head.
- **Weaknesses.** Doesn't scale past a handful of people. Context is lost every time the founder steps away. Follow-up drops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Encode the loops the founder already runs in their head as Programs, so the team compounds leverage instead of rebuilding context each week.

_Optional fourth row — agent infrastructure (LangChain, Mem0, Glean). Call out if needed: these are platform components, not startup operating systems._

### Pricing strategy

Per-team pricing that unlocks the runtime on every plan. Free tier for solo founders removes the adoption barrier. Team tier is priced flat per company (not per seat) so the OS framing matches the billing — one license runs the company. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra). [TBD — confirm per-team vs. per-user; pitch-deck slide 9 flags this as open.]

### SWOT analysis

**Strengths.**
- Clear positioning — "OS for startups" is a category the market is starting to name but no one owns yet.
- Working runtime already in production (see SPEC.md `v0.1.0` entities).
- Founder-built with no external funding for 12 months — strong capital efficiency signal.
- 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated the core problem.

**Weaknesses.**
- Team of one. Execution bandwidth is finite.
- Pre-revenue. No live pricing data yet.
- Broad scope (product, GTM, support, ops) risks being seen as "doing everything" before any surface is deeply loved.
- Category ambiguity — "operating system" is a claim that requires proof.

**Opportunities.**
- Coding agents are hitting mainstream adoption (75% of Linear enterprise workspaces run coding agents; agents authored ~25% of new issues last quarter). The coordination layer is the next bottleneck.
- Modern APIs now reach inbox, repo, CRM, and error log — the integration surface is finally addressable.
- Every YC batch adds thousands of small teams to the target market annually.
- Answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity) create a new inbound channel that rewards structured, citable content.

**Threats.**
- Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) expand scope into company operations.
- A well-funded competitor lands the "startup OS" category first.
- Platform risk — core connected tools (GitHub, Linear) restrict integration surface or build similar features natively.
- Macro tightening slows seed fundraising and small-team formation.

#### How we'll address each weakness and threat

- **Team of one / bandwidth** — close seed round, make first 1–2 senior hires, lean heavily on Lightfast itself to compound leverage.
- **Pre-revenue / category ambiguity** — ship Team tier to paid design partners before broad launch; let one surface (Opportunities or Memory) carry the initial wedge story.
- **Horizontal agents expanding** — compose with them, not against them; own the Programs / Opportunities layer they don't build.
- **Platform risk** — vendor-abstract every integration (the `@vendor/*` pattern already enforces this) and invest in webhooks-first ingestion that degrades gracefully.
- **Macro** — capital efficiency is already demonstrated (12 months at $0); the seed ask is sized for 12–18 months of runway.

---

## Risk management

|---|---|---|
| Key person risk — founder incapacitated | Low | Document all systems, automate backups, set up access recovery for all critical accounts. |
| Data loss from customer-tenant data | Low | Tenant isolation, DB backups, disaster-recovery runbook. [TBD — formal runbook.] |
| Security breach / credential leak | Medium | Vendor abstractions with scoped credentials, secret rotation, [TBD — formal security review cadence]. |
| Platform dependency (GitHub / Linear API changes) | Medium | Vendor abstraction layer `@vendor/*`; webhook-first with graceful degradation. |
| Fundraise fails / runway shortens | Medium | Ship paid Team tier early; capital efficiency demonstrated. |
| Competitor lands "startup OS" category first | Medium | Ship faster on Programs + Opportunities; build AEO distribution moat. |
| AI provider outage (Anthropic, OpenAI) | Medium | Multi-provider abstraction; degrade to cached results where possible. |
| Regulatory change (AU privacy, EU AI Act) | Low | Privacy-by-default architecture already in place (no training on customer data). |

Pre-hire stage — founder is sole operator. [TBD — Once a co-founder or senior hire is in place, document handover of ops, credentials, and decision-making. Consider keyman life / TPD insurance as part of this.]

### Laws we'll need to comply with

- Australian Privacy Act 1988 (customer data handling).
- Spam Act 2003 (marketing communications).
- Consumer law (Australian Consumer Law under Competition and Consumer Act 2010).
- GDPR (any EU-resident users).
- Fair Work Act (once first employee is hired).
- [TBD — industry-specific laws if selling into regulated verticals, e.g. healthcare, finance.]

- **Macro** — capital efficiency is already demonstrated (12 months at $0); the seed ask is sized for 12–18 months of runway.

---

## Risk management

### Risk assessment

> Draft — please review likelihoods and add anything I've missed.

| Risk / impact | Likelihood | What we'll do to reduce this risk |
|---|---|---|
| Key person risk — founder incapacitated | Low | Document all systems, automate backups, set up access recovery for all critical accounts. |
| Data loss from customer-tenant data | Low | Tenant isolation, DB backups, disaster-recovery runbook. [TBD — formal runbook.] |
| Security breach / credential leak | Medium | Vendor abstractions with scoped credentials, secret rotation, [TBD — formal security review cadence]. |
| Platform dependency (GitHub / Linear API changes) | Medium | Vendor abstraction layer `@vendor/*`; webhook-first with graceful degradation. |
| Fundraise fails / runway shortens | Medium | Ship paid Team tier early; capital efficiency demonstrated. |
| Competitor lands "startup OS" category first | Medium | Ship faster on Programs + Opportunities; build AEO distribution moat. |
| AI provider outage (Anthropic, OpenAI) | Medium | Multi-provider abstraction; degrade to cached results where possible. |
| Regulatory change (AU privacy, EU AI Act) | Low | Privacy-by-default architecture already in place (no training on customer data). |

### Succession plan

Pre-hire stage — founder is sole operator. [TBD — Once a co-founder or senior hire is in place, document handover of ops, credentials, and decision-making. Consider keyman life / TPD insurance as part of this.]

### Laws we'll need to comply with

- Australian Privacy Act 1988 (customer data handling).
- Spam Act 2003 (marketing communications).
- Consumer law (Australian Consumer Law under Competition and Consumer Act 2010).
- GDPR (any EU-resident users).
- Fair Work Act (once first employee is hired).
- [TBD — industry-specific laws if selling into regulated verticals, e.g. healthcare, finance.]

input.old_string:
### Pricing strategy

Per-team pricing that unlocks the runtime on every plan. Free tier for solo founders removes the adoption barrier. Team tier is priced flat per company (not per seat) so the OS framing matches the billing — one license runs the company. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra). [TBD — confirm per-team vs. per-user; pitch-deck slide 9 flags this as open.]
input.new_string:
### Pricing strategy

Per-user pricing at $50 / user / month on the Team tier. Free Solo tier removes the adoption barrier and lets individual founders adopt before paying. Team tier scales linearly with headcount — the more the company grows, the more Lightfast compounds across the org. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra) with custom pricing. Rationale: per-user aligns revenue with actual usage density and matches the mental model of every comparable developer tool (Linear, Notion, Intercom).

75-
76-**Our target market.** Teams of 1 to 30 building software today. Solo founders, seed-stage teams, and Series A teams shipping daily on a fragmented tool stack.
77-
78-**Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them and routes the work.
79-

Locked into the plan: Pty Ltd (VIC), home-office Melbourne, $50/user/mo pricing, $500K SAFE, Luna Startup Lawyers, no insurance yet, trade mark unfiled, $25K MRR Y1 target.

**Plain-text asks (please paste what you have):**
1. Registered business name (e.g. "Lightfast Pty Ltd")
2. ABN
3. ACN
4. Date of registration

Next decision-y batch — hiring, design partners, use of funds, and Y3 scale:

questions.question: What's the hiring plan over the next 12-18 months (post seed close)?

options.label: 1 senior engineer + design contractor (Recommended)
options.description: Lean — founder stays on product, senior engineer ships, design is fractional. Matches a $500K / 12-18mo runway.

options.label: 2 senior engineers + design contractor
options.description: More engineering bandwidth — tighter runway but faster delivery on Programs + Opportunities.

options.label: 1 engineer + 1 GTM/DevRel hire
options.description: Split between build and distribution — good if outbound / AEO is the main growth bet.

options.label: No hires — solo through seed
options.description: Extend runway, compound on Lightfast itself, hire only after Series A is in sight.

questions.question: What's the design-partner / paying-customer status today?

options.label: Pre-any-customers
options.description: No design partners signed yet. Plan documents this and sets 'first 5 design partners' as Y1 milestone.

options.label: 1-3 design partners (unpaid)
options.description: Early design partners testing the product — I'll capture count without naming them.

options.label: 5+ design partners
options.description: Broader pilot cohort — meaningful validation signal to include in the plan.

options.label: Paying customers already
options.description: Live revenue — tell me count + approx MRR so I can put actual numbers in Current Finances.

questions.question: Pick the use-of-funds split for the $500K SAFE (best estimate):

options.label: 60% engineering / 20% infra / 10% distribution / 10% legal+ops (Recommended)
options.description: Engineering-heavy. Matches lean hiring + known infra costs + trade mark + insurance + operating runway.

options.label: 50% engineering / 20% infra / 20% distribution / 10% legal+ops
options.description: More into distribution (AEO content, founder outbound, partner listings) if growth is the constraint.

options.label: 40% engineering / 15% infra / 25% distribution / 20% legal+ops
options.description: Heavier on GTM + legal — relevant if Scale/enterprise motion starts early.

options.label: Founder decides later
options.description: Mark as TBD and leave for investor conversations / board discussions.

questions.question: What's the Y3 target I should lock in?

options.label: $1M ARR, team of ~8, Series A raise (Recommended)
options.description: Standard seed-to-Series-A path. $1M ARR = ~165 paying teams at 10-user avg. Team of 8 = eng-heavy with first GTM + support.

options.label: $3M ARR, team of ~15, Series A closed
options.description: Aggressive — requires strong NRR from land-and-expand plus meaningful Scale-tier deals.

options.label: $500K ARR, team of ~5, extend runway
options.description: Conservative — treat Y3 as capital-efficient, prove the model before Series A.

options.label: Mark as TBD
options.description: Leave Y3 goals as directional and flesh out in a later revision.

options.label: 50% engineering / 20% infra / 20% distribution / 10% legal+ops
options.description: More into distribution (AEO content, founder outbound, partner listings) if growth is the constraint.

options.label: 40% engineering / 15% infra / 25% distribution / 20% legal+ops
options.description: Heavier on GTM + legal — relevant if Scale/enterprise motion starts early.

options.label: Founder decides later
options.description: Mark as TBD and leave for investor conversations / board discussions.

questions.question: What's the Y3 target I should lock in?

options.label: $1M ARR, team of ~8, Series A raise (Recommended)
options.description: Standard seed-to-Series-A path. $1M ARR = ~165 paying teams at 10-user avg. Team of 8 = eng-heavy with first GTM + support.

options.label: $3M ARR, team of ~15, Series A closed
options.description: Aggressive — requires strong NRR from land-and-expand plus meaningful Scale-tier deals.

options.label: $500K ARR, team of ~5, extend runway
options.description: Conservative — treat Y3 as capital-efficient, prove the model before Series A.

options.label: Mark as TBD
options.description: Leave Y3 goals as directional and flesh out in a later revision.

answers.What's the Y3 target I should lock in?: $1M ARR, team of ~8, Series A raise (Recommended)

> **All figures below are rough directional scaffolding** derived from the $500K SAFE, $50/user/month pricing, $25K MRR Y1 target, and $1M ARR Y3 target. Accountant to finalize. AUD.

### Balance forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total assets (cash + receivables + equipment) | ~$250K | ~$400K | ~$1.2M |
| Total liabilities (SAFE → equity on Series A, accounts payable) | ~$520K | ~$540K | ~$50K |
| Net assets | ~-$270K | ~-$140K | ~$1.15M |

_SAFE appears as liability until Series A triggers conversion. Post-conversion in Y3, balance sheet strengthens materially._

### Profit and loss forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross revenue / net sales | ~$120K (ramp $0 → $25K MRR) | ~$500K (ramp $25K → $60K MRR) | ~$1.0M (ramp to $1M ARR run-rate) |
| Cost of revenue (infra + AI tokens) | ~$60K | ~$120K | ~$200K |
| Gross profit | ~$60K | ~$380K | ~$800K |
| Operating expenses (payroll + legal + distribution) | ~$420K | ~$650K | ~$1.4M |
| Net profit / loss | ~-$360K | ~-$270K | ~-$600K (investable loss; Series A funds growth) |

_Y1-Y3 are growth-investment years. Gross margin improves from ~50% to ~80% as infra amortizes over a larger user base._

### Cash flow forecast (Year 1)

Assumes $500K SAFE closes early in the year. Monthly burn ~$35K average, partially offset by revenue ramp. AUD.

input.old_string:
### Succession plan

Pre-hire stage — founder is sole operator. [TBD — Once a co-founder or senior hire is in place, document handover of ops, credentials, and decision-making. Consider keyman life / TPD insurance as part of this.]
input.new_string:
### Succession plan

Solo-founder stage. The founder is the sole operator and sole director, which concentrates key-person risk. Mitigations:

- **Credentials and access recovery.** All critical accounts (domain registrar, cloud providers, DNS, code repositories, payments, banking) use the founder's primary identity with a documented recovery procedure. A sealed credential-recovery kit is kept with the lawyer (Luna Startup Lawyers) to be opened only on incapacity.
- **Staged advisor handover.** In the event the founder is incapacitated, Luna Startup Lawyers acts as first contact to notify investors, the advisor, and any paying customers, and initiates a wind-down or sale process consistent with SAFE terms and fiduciary obligations.
- **Key-person insurance.** Life / TPD insurance on the founder to be taken out once revenue is material enough to warrant the premium (target: post-Series A, or earlier if investors request it as a condition of the SAFE).
- **First senior hire.** Once the first senior engineer is onboarded post-seed, a continuity plan is documented so they can keep the runtime operational for at least 90 days without founder involvement.
- **Voluntary step-down.** If the founder chooses to step down, preference is given to the next senior operator (likely the first senior engineer hire) with a standard vesting-acceleration and share-buyback process governed by the shareholder agreement.

lines: ### Succession plan

-Pre-hire stage — founder is sole operator. [TBD — Once a co-founder or senior hire is in place, document handover of ops, credentials, and decision-making. Consider keyman life / TPD insurance as part of this.]
+Solo-founder stage. The founder is the sole operator and sole director, which concentrates key-person risk. Mitigations:

+- **Credentials and access recovery.** All critical accounts (domain registrar, cloud providers, DNS, code repositories, payments, banking) use the founder's primary identity with a documented recovery procedure. A sealed credential-recovery kit is kept with the lawyer (Luna Startup Lawyers) to be opened only on incapacity.

+- **Staged advisor handover.** In the event the founder is incapacitated, Luna Startup Lawyers acts as first contact to notify investors, the advisor, and any paying customers, and initiates a wind-down or sale process consistent with SAFE terms and fiduciary obligations.

+- **Key-person insurance.** Life / TPD insurance on the founder to be taken out once revenue is material enough to warrant the premium (target: post-Series A, or earlier if investors request it as a condition of the SAFE).

+- **First senior hire.** Once the first senior engineer is onboarded post-seed, a continuity plan is documented so they can keep the runtime operational for at least 90 days without founder involvement.

+- **Voluntary step-down.** If the founder chooses to step down, preference is given to the next senior operator (likely the first senior engineer hire) with a standard vesting-acceleration and share-buyback process governed by the shareholder agreement.
### Laws we'll need to comply with
- Australian Privacy Act 1988 (customer data handling).

78	## The business
79	
80	### Plan summary
81	
82	> Complete this section last — summarise from the rest of the plan.
83	
84	**What our business does.** Lightfast is an operating system for startups. It runs the work between tools — Programs, Processes, Memory, and Opportunities — so small teams can ship product, launch, support customers, and close the loop without stitching context by hand.
85	
86	**Our target market.** Teams of 1 to 30 building software today. Solo founders, seed-stage teams, and Series A teams shipping daily on a fragmented tool stack.
87	
88	**Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them and routes the work.
89	
90	**Our business goals.**
91	- Close $500K SAFE seed round for 12–18 months of runway.
92	- Ship v1 runtime — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities — to design-partner teams.
93	- Reach $25K MRR by end of Year 1.
94	
95	**Our history.** 12 months of development. 3,930 commits. $0 external funding to date. Prior experience across AI infrastructure, computational design, and fabrication systems. 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated context loss as a recurring top-pain point.
96	
97	### Our why
98	
99	Founders spend their day stitching context between tools. Code is cheaper than ever to write, but the work that surrounds it — deciding what to build next, triaging feedback, closing the loop on support, running GTM, catching errors — is still manual. Lightfast exists because coordination is the next bottleneck, and no one is building the layer that runs it.
100	
101	### Our vision
102	
103	Every startup runs on Lightfast. The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the company moving — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops — with the same compounding leverage that coding agents gave engineers.
104	
105	### Our mission
106	
107	Give small teams one system to run the company. Unify company knowledge into expressible logic that is reusable anywhere. Put the startup-building lifecycle on autopilot so founders spend time on judgment, not follow-up.
108	
109	### Operations
110	
111	#### Products and services
112	
113	| Product / service | Description | Unit price (incl. GST) |
114	|---|---|---|
115	| Solo | 1 user · 3 Pipelines · Core Memory | Free |
116	| Team | Up to 30 users · all seven Pipelines · agent + MCP access | $50 / user / month |
117	| Scale | 30+ users · SSO · audit log · dedicated infra | Custom |
118	
119	#### Production
120	
121	Lightfast is a SaaS product built on a pnpm Turborepo monorepo. Runtime is Next.js (apps), tRPC + Inngest (APIs + workflows), Postgres via Drizzle, Upstash Redis, and vendor-abstracted integrations with GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, and similar tools. Hosted on Vercel (web + platform) with microfrontends composition.
122	
123	We produce the product in-house. No external production suppliers beyond cloud infrastructure providers (Vercel, database host, Upstash, Inngest, Anthropic, OpenAI).
124	
125	#### Sales and distribution channels
126	
127	| Channel | Used for | Details |
128	|---|---|---|
129	| Direct (website) | Self-serve signup for Solo + Team | lightfast.ai — primary channel |
130	| Outbound / founder-led | Early design partners on Team / Scale | Founder-led conversations with seed and Series A teams |
131	| Content + docs | Inbound via AEO + SEO | Changelog, blog, docs indexed by answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) |
132	| Partner integrations | Distribution through connected tools | Listings / marketplace placements where available (GitHub, Linear, etc.) |
133	
134	#### Digital technology
135	
136	Customer-facing stack: Next.js web app, TypeScript SDK, MCP server, REST API, webhooks. Internal stack: Postgres, Redis, Inngest (background jobs), ngrok (dev), Vercel (hosting). Analytics + observability: [TBD — confirm PostHog, Sentry, other]. Accounting + billing: [TBD — Stripe for billing; Xero / accountant for books].
137	
138	#### Information management
139	
140	Customer data is isolated per tenant. Events are stored as immutable facts with causal ordering. Semantic embeddings and a temporal graph sit on top. Customer data is never used for model training. Backups run via database provider standard tooling. [TBD — document backup cadence and retention policy.]
141	
142	#### Assets
143	
144	**Premises.** Home office in Melbourne. No commercial lease. Home-office deduction applies for tax purposes (to be documented with accountant).
145	
146	**Stock and inventory.** Not applicable. Lightfast is a software business with no physical inventory.
147	
148	**Equipment.**
149	
150	No material equipment held by the entity. Founder currently operates on personal hardware; a company laptop is to be purchased post-seed close as a Year-1 capital expense. Cloud infrastructure replaces traditional server / storage assets.
151	
152	**Intellectual property.** Trade mark not yet filed — registration of the "Lightfast" name and logo with IP Australia is a near-term action (to be handled with Luna Startup Lawyers). Codebase copyright is held by the entity and is Apache 2 licensed per the recent relicense. Domain `lightfast.ai` is held by the entity.
153	
154	### Key people
155	
156	#### Our staff
157	
158	| Role | Name | Skills and experience |
159	|---|---|---|
160	| Founder & CEO | Jeevan Pillay | 12 months building Lightfast — 3,930 commits across ingestion, retrieval, orchestration. Prior: AI infrastructure, computational design, code-to-fabrication systems. Led technical campaigns generating $1M+ revenue. |
161	| Senior engineer #1 (planned) | [TBD] | First full-stack / backend hire post-seed. Expected to own core runtime (Processes, Memory) and integration surface. |
162	| Senior engineer #2 (planned) | [TBD] | Second engineering hire post-seed. Expected to own Programs + Opportunities surface and SDK / MCP server. |
163	| Design contractor (planned) | [TBD] | Fractional design support for product surfaces and marketing site. |
164	
165	#### Sources of advice and support
166	
167	| Support type | Name | Skills and experience |
168	|---|---|---|
169	| Lawyer (startup / tech) | Luna Startup Lawyers | Specialist Australian startup-stage firm. Advises on SAFE docs, IP, T&Cs, and privacy policy. Engagement expected to expand into accounting / bookkeeping in the coming months. |
170	| Accountant / bookkeeper | [Luna Startup Lawyers — planned engagement] | To be engaged in the coming months for BAS, tax, and books. |
171	
172	#### Skill and staff retention strategies
173	
174	Pre-hire stage. To be populated once the first hires are made. Anchor principles: equity participation for early hires, remote-first, async default, strong documentation culture so context carries across the team rather than living in heads.
175	
176	---
177	
178	## The market
179	
180	### The problem
181	
182	Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company. Coding agents flattened the cost of shipping. What remains is everything around code — priorities, support, launches, bugs, follow-up — and it still lives across disconnected tools. Founders and small teams spend their time stitching context together and keeping work from slipping. The product is faster to build. The company is still manual.
183	
184	### Our solution
185	
186	Lightfast gives startups one system to run the company — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops. Four primitives, built once, composable forever:
187	
188	- **Programs** — plain-language operating spec for the company.
189	- **Processes** — managed workflows with clear state.
190	- **Memory** — shared context across the work, with citations back to source events.
191	- **Opportunities** — the system surfaces the highest-leverage next action across product, growth, support, and ops.
192	
193	The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the work moving.
194	
195	### Our target market
196	
197	Teams of 1 to 30 shipping software today.
198	
199	- **Solo founders** — building a product alone, context lives in their head and browser tabs.
200	- **2–10 person seed teams** — first hires made, tools multiplying, coordination cost rising.
201	- **10–30 person Series A teams** — every function represented, but glue-work absorbs the founder.
202	
203	**How many.**
204	- 80K+ YC applications annually.
205	- ~50K US seed-stage companies funded annually.
206	- Millions of solo builders globally.
207	
208	**Why underserved.** Existing tooling is built for one function at a time. Small teams carry the overlap themselves.
209	
210	### Advertising and promotion
211	
212	| Channel | Used for | Details |
213	|---|---|---|
214	| Content / AEO | Inbound via answer engines and search | Changelog, blog, docs structured for citation by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews. Lightfast's internal `lightfast-aeo` skill governs voice and structure. |
215	| Founder-led outbound | Seed round + design partners | Direct conversations with seed / Series A founders in network. |
216	| X / social | Build-in-public loop | Ship cadence visible; attract engineers and founders. |
217	| Partner ecosystem | Distribution via connected tools | GitHub Marketplace, Linear integrations directory, etc. |
218	| Community / events | [TBD — YC, local Melbourne startup community, founder dinners] | |
219	
220	### The competition
221	
222	Top categories we compete against.
223	
224	#### Competitor 1 — Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex)
225	
226	- **Strengths.** Powerful general agents. Deep Anthropic / OpenAI distribution. Flatten the cost of writing software.
227	- **Weaknesses.** General-purpose. Execute tasks; do not run a company's operating loops. No native Programs, Processes, or Opportunities surface.
228	- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast is the layer above: it decides what the company should do next, then routes work — including to these agents where appropriate. We compose with them, not against them on code generation.
229	
230	#### Competitor 2 — Point solutions (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog)
231	
232	- **Strengths.** Deep ownership of one pipeline each (issues, docs, support, analytics). Loved by users within their domain.
233	- **Weaknesses.** Each owns one pipeline. The work between them falls on the founder. None coordinate across product, GTM, support, and ops.
234	- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast doesn't replace them. It observes them, remembers across them, and acts through them — with citations back to the source events.
235	
236	#### Competitor 3 — Status quo (founder + tabs + context switching)
237	
238	- **Strengths.** Zero vendor cost. Fully flexible. Founder knows everything in their head.
239	- **Weaknesses.** Doesn't scale past a handful of people. Context is lost every time the founder steps away. Follow-up drops.
240	- **What we'll do differently.** Encode the loops the founder already runs in their head as Programs, so the team compounds leverage instead of rebuilding context each week.
241	
242	_Optional fourth row — agent infrastructure (LangChain, Mem0, Glean). Call out if needed: these are platform components, not startup operating systems._
243	
244	### Pricing strategy
245	
246	Per-user pricing at $50 / user / month on the Team tier. Free Solo tier removes the adoption barrier and lets individual founders adopt before paying. Team tier scales linearly with headcount — the more the company grows, the more Lightfast compounds across the org. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra) with custom pricing. Rationale: per-user aligns revenue with actual usage density and matches the mental model of every comparable developer tool (Linear, Notion, Intercom).
247	
248	### SWOT analysis
249	
250	**Strengths.**
251	- Clear positioning — "OS for startups" is a category the market is starting to name but no one owns yet.
252	- Working runtime already in production (see SPEC.md `v0.1.0` entities).
253	- Founder-built with no external funding for 12 months — strong capital efficiency signal.
254	- 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated the core problem.
255	
256	**Weaknesses.**
257	- Team of one. Execution bandwidth is finite.
258	- Pre-revenue. No live pricing data yet.
259	- Broad scope (product, GTM, support, ops) risks being seen as "doing everything" before any surface is deeply loved.
260	- Category ambiguity — "operating system" is a claim that requires proof.
261	
262	**Opportunities.**
263	- Coding agents are hitting mainstream adoption (75% of Linear enterprise workspaces run coding agents; agents authored ~25% of new issues last quarter). The coordination layer is the next bottleneck.
264	- Modern APIs now reach inbox, repo, CRM, and error log — the integration surface is finally addressable.
265	- Every YC batch adds thousands of small teams to the target market annually.
266	- Answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity) create a new inbound channel that rewards structured, citable content.
267	
268	**Threats.**
269	- Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) expand scope into company operations.
270	- A well-funded competitor lands the "startup OS" category first.
271	- Platform risk — core connected tools (GitHub, Linear) restrict integration surface or build similar features natively.
272	- Macro tightening slows seed fundraising and small-team formation.
273	
274	#### How we'll address each weakness and threat
275	
276	- **Team of one / bandwidth** — close seed round, make first 1–2 senior hires, lean heavily on Lightfast itself to compound leverage.
277	- **Pre-revenue / category ambiguity** — ship Team tier to paid design partners before broad launch; let one surface (Opportunities or Memory) carry the initial wedge story.
278	- **Horizontal agents expanding** — compose with them, not against them; own the Programs / Opportunities layer they don't build.
279	- **Platform risk** — vendor-abstract every integration (the `@vendor/*` pattern already enforces this) and invest in webhooks-first ingestion that degrades gracefully.
280	- **Macro** — capital efficiency is already demonstrated (12 months at $0); the seed ask is sized for 12–18 months of runway.
281	
282	---
283	
284	## Risk management
285	
286	### Risk assessment
287	
288	> Draft — please review likelihoods and add anything I've missed.
289	
290	| Risk / impact | Likelihood | What we'll do to reduce this risk |
291	|---|---|---|
292	| Key person risk — founder incapacitated | Low | Document all systems, automate backups, set up access recovery for all critical accounts. |
293	| Data loss from customer-tenant data | Low | Tenant isolation, DB backups, disaster-recovery runbook. [TBD — formal runbook.] |
294	| Security breach / credential leak | Medium | Vendor abstractions with scoped credentials, secret rotation, [TBD — formal security review cadence]. |
295	| Platform dependency (GitHub / Linear API changes) | **High** | Vendor abstraction layer `@vendor/*`; webhook-first with graceful degradation. Monitor API deprecations proactively. Maintain fallback polling paths. |
296	| Fundraise fails / runway shortens | Medium | Ship paid Team tier early; capital efficiency demonstrated. |
297	| Competitor lands "startup OS" category first | **High** | Ship faster on Programs + Opportunities; build AEO distribution moat; lock early design partners with multi-year agreements where sensible. |
298	| AI provider outage / pricing shock (Anthropic, OpenAI) | **High** | Multi-provider abstraction; degrade to cached results where possible. Hedge spend across providers. Watch token-pricing trends and build in price-pass-through on Team + Scale tiers. |
299	| Regulatory change (AU privacy, EU AI Act) | Low | Privacy-by-default architecture already in place (no training on customer data). |
300	
301	### Insurance
302	
303	Nothing currently in place. Required before paid customers onboard.
304	
305	| Insurance type | Status | Action |
306	|---|---|---|
307	| Professional Indemnity | Not in place | Required before Team tier paid launch — covers claims arising from professional services. |
308	| Cyber insurance | Not in place | Required before Team tier paid launch — covers data breach / credential leak incidents. Strongly recommended given customer data custody. |
309	| Public Liability | Not in place | Standard Australian business cover — lower priority for pure SaaS with no physical premises visitors. |
310	| Business interruption | Not in place | Review once revenue is material. |
311	| Workers compensation | Not applicable yet | Required by law once first employee is hired in Victoria. |
312	
313	### Succession plan
314	
315	Solo-founder stage. The founder is the sole operator and sole director, which concentrates key-person risk. Mitigations:
316	
317	- **Credentials and access recovery.** All critical accounts (domain registrar, cloud providers, DNS, code repositories, payments, banking) use the founder's primary identity with a documented recovery procedure. A sealed credential-recovery kit is kept with the lawyer (Luna Startup Lawyers) to be opened only on incapacity.
318	- **Staged advisor handover.** In the event the founder is incapacitated, Luna Startup Lawyers acts as first contact to notify investors, the advisor, and any paying customers, and initiates a wind-down or sale process consistent with SAFE terms and fiduciary obligations.
319	- **Key-person insurance.** Life / TPD insurance on the founder to be taken out once revenue is material enough to warrant the premium (target: post-Series A, or earlier if investors request it as a condition of the SAFE).
320	- **First senior hire.** Once the first senior engineer is onboarded post-seed, a continuity plan is documented so they can keep the runtime operational for at least 90 days without founder involvement.
321	- **Voluntary step-down.** If the founder chooses to step down, preference is given to the next senior operator (likely the first senior engineer hire) with a standard vesting-acceleration and share-buyback process governed by the shareholder agreement.
322	
323	### Laws we'll need to comply with
324	
325	- Australian Privacy Act 1988 (customer data handling).
326	- Spam Act 2003 (marketing communications).
327	- Consumer law (Australian Consumer Law under Competition and Consumer Act 2010).
328	- GDPR (any EU-resident users).
329	- Fair Work Act (once first employee is hired).
330	- [TBD — industry-specific laws if selling into regulated verticals, e.g. healthcare, finance.]
331	
332	---
333	
334	## Goals and actions
335	
336	### Goals for next year
337	
338	> Draft — please confirm or edit each goal and the actions underneath.
339	
340	| Goal | Actions to achieve goal | Due date | Who's responsible |
341	|---|---|---|---|
342	| Close seed round | Finalise deck · run founder intros · sign SAFEs · update cap table | [TBD — Q3 2026?] | Jeevan |
343	| Ship v1 Team tier to paid design partners | Complete Programs + Opportunities runtime · convert existing 1-3 unpaid design partners · onboard 5-10 paid design partners · hire 2 senior engineers + design contractor post-seed · iterate weekly on feedback | [TBD] | Jeevan + first eng hires |
344	| Reach $25K MRR by end of Year 1 | Launch public pricing · publish changelog + blog cadence · activate AEO distribution · founder-led outbound · convert design partners to paid · land ~50 paying teams at average 10-user density | [TBD — end of 2026 / mid-2027 depending on fiscal year] | Jeevan |
345	
346	### Goals for the next 3 years
347	
348	| Goal | Actions to achieve goal | Due date | Who's responsible |
349	|---|---|---|---|

352	| Team of ~8 people across engineering, GTM, support | Seed eng hires (×2) in place · add first GTM / DevRel hire · add support / ops hire · add 2-3 more engineers post-Series A | [TBD] | Jeevan |
353	
354	---
355	
356	## The finances
357	
358	> All dollar figures are placeholders. Work with your accountant + the pitch-deck finance model to populate.

## Key details
