---

## The business

### Plan summary

> Complete this section last — summarise from the rest of the plan.

**What our business does.** Lightfast is an operating system for startups. It runs the work between tools — Programs, Processes, Memory, and Opportunities — so small teams can ship product, launch, support customers, and close the loop without stitching context by hand.

**Our target market.** Teams of 1 to 30 building software today. Solo founders, seed-stage teams, and Series A teams shipping daily on a fragmented tool stack.

**Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them and routes the work.

**Our business goals.**
- Close $500K SAFE seed round for 12–18 months of runway.
- Ship v1 runtime — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities — to design-partner teams.
- Reach $25K MRR by end of Year 1.

**Our history.** 12 months of development. 3,930 commits. $0 external funding to date. Prior experience across AI infrastructure, computational design, and fabrication systems. 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated context loss as a recurring top-pain point.

### Our why

Founders spend their day stitching context between tools. Code is cheaper than ever to write, but the work that surrounds it — deciding what to build next, triaging feedback, closing the loop on support, running GTM, catching errors — is still manual. Lightfast exists because coordination is the next bottleneck, and no one is building the layer that runs it.

### Our vision

Every startup runs on Lightfast. The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the company moving — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops — with the same compounding leverage that coding agents gave engineers.

### Our mission

Give small teams one system to run the company. Unify company knowledge into expressible logic that is reusable anywhere. Put the startup-building lifecycle on autopilot so founders spend time on judgment, not follow-up.

### Operations

#### Products and services

| Product / service | Description | Unit price (incl. GST) |
|---|---|---|
| Solo | 1 user · 3 Pipelines · Core Memory | Free |
| Team | Up to 30 users · all seven Pipelines · agent + MCP access | $50 / user / month |
| Scale | 30+ users · SSO · audit log · dedicated infra | Custom |

#### Production

Lightfast is a SaaS product built on a pnpm Turborepo monorepo. Runtime is Next.js (apps), tRPC + Inngest (APIs + workflows), Postgres via Drizzle, Upstash Redis, and vendor-abstracted integrations with GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, and similar tools. Hosted on Vercel (web + platform) with microfrontends composition.

We produce the product in-house. No external production suppliers beyond cloud infrastructure providers (Vercel, database host, Upstash, Inngest, Anthropic, OpenAI).

#### Sales and distribution channels

| Channel | Used for | Details |
|---|---|---|
| Direct (website) | Self-serve signup for Solo + Team | lightfast.ai — primary channel |
| Outbound / founder-led | Early design partners on Team / Scale | Founder-led conversations with seed and Series A teams |
| Content + docs | Inbound via AEO + SEO | Changelog, blog, docs indexed by answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) |
| Partner integrations | Distribution through connected tools | Listings / marketplace placements where available (GitHub, Linear, etc.) |

#### Digital technology

Customer-facing stack: Next.js web app, TypeScript SDK, MCP server, REST API, webhooks. Internal stack: Postgres, Redis, Inngest (background jobs), ngrok (dev), Vercel (hosting). Analytics + observability: [TBD — confirm PostHog, Sentry, other]. Accounting + billing: [TBD — Stripe for billing; Xero / accountant for books].

#### Information management

Customer data is isolated per tenant. Events are stored as immutable facts with causal ordering. Semantic embeddings and a temporal graph sit on top. Customer data is never used for model training. Backups run via database provider standard tooling. [TBD — document backup cadence and retention policy.]

#### Assets

**Premises.** Home office in Melbourne. No commercial lease. Home-office deduction applies for tax purposes (to be documented with accountant).

**Stock and inventory.** Not applicable. Lightfast is a software business with no physical inventory.

**Equipment.**

No material equipment held by the entity. Founder currently operates on personal hardware; a company laptop is to be purchased post-seed close as a Year-1 capital expense. Cloud infrastructure replaces traditional server / storage assets.

**Intellectual property.** Trade mark not yet filed — registration of the "Lightfast" name and logo with IP Australia is a near-term action (to be handled with Luna Startup Lawyers). Codebase copyright is held by the entity and is Apache 2 licensed per the recent relicense. Domain `lightfast.ai` is held by the entity.

### Key people

#### Our staff

| Role | Name | Skills and experience |
|---|---|---|
| Founder & CEO | Jeevan Pillay | 12 months building Lightfast — 3,930 commits across ingestion, retrieval, orchestration. Prior: AI infrastructure, computational design, code-to-fabrication systems. Led technical campaigns generating $1M+ revenue. |
| Senior engineer #1 (planned) | [TBD] | First full-stack / backend hire post-seed. Expected to own core runtime (Processes, Memory) and integration surface. |
| Senior engineer #2 (planned) | [TBD] | Second engineering hire post-seed. Expected to own Programs + Opportunities surface and SDK / MCP server. |
| Design contractor (planned) | [TBD] | Fractional design support for product surfaces and marketing site. |

#### Sources of advice and support

| Support type | Name | Skills and experience |
|---|---|---|
| Lawyer (startup / tech) | Luna Startup Lawyers | Specialist Australian startup-stage firm. Advises on SAFE docs, IP, T&Cs, and privacy policy. Engagement expected to expand into accounting / bookkeeping in the coming months. |
| Accountant / bookkeeper | [Luna Startup Lawyers — planned engagement] | To be engaged in the coming months for BAS, tax, and books. |

#### Skill and staff retention strategies

Pre-hire stage. To be populated once the first hires are made. Anchor principles: equity participation for early hires, remote-first, async default, strong documentation culture so context carries across the team rather than living in heads.

---

## The market

### The problem

Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company. Coding agents flattened the cost of shipping. What remains is everything around code — priorities, support, launches, bugs, follow-up — and it still lives across disconnected tools. Founders and small teams spend their time stitching context together and keeping work from slipping. The product is faster to build. The company is still manual.

### Our solution

Lightfast gives startups one system to run the company — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops. Four primitives, built once, composable forever:

- **Programs** — plain-language operating spec for the company.
- **Processes** — managed workflows with clear state.
- **Memory** — shared context across the work, with citations back to source events.
- **Opportunities** — the system surfaces the highest-leverage next action across product, growth, support, and ops.

The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the work moving.

### Our target market

Teams of 1 to 30 shipping software today.

- **Solo founders** — building a product alone, context lives in their head and browser tabs.
- **2–10 person seed teams** — first hires made, tools multiplying, coordination cost rising.
- **10–30 person Series A teams** — every function represented, but glue-work absorbs the founder.

**How many.**
- 80K+ YC applications annually.
- ~50K US seed-stage companies funded annually.
- Millions of solo builders globally.

**Why underserved.** Existing tooling is built for one function at a time. Small teams carry the overlap themselves.

### Advertising and promotion

| Channel | Used for | Details |
|---|---|---|
| Content / AEO | Inbound via answer engines and search | Changelog, blog, docs structured for citation by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews. Lightfast's internal `lightfast-aeo` skill governs voice and structure. |
| Founder-led outbound | Seed round + design partners | Direct conversations with seed / Series A founders in network. |
| X / social | Build-in-public loop | Ship cadence visible; attract engineers and founders. |
| Partner ecosystem | Distribution via connected tools | GitHub Marketplace, Linear integrations directory, etc. |
| Community / events | [TBD — YC, local Melbourne startup community, founder dinners] | |

### The competition

Top categories we compete against.

#### Competitor 1 — Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex)

- **Strengths.** Powerful general agents. Deep Anthropic / OpenAI distribution. Flatten the cost of writing software.
- **Weaknesses.** General-purpose. Execute tasks; do not run a company's operating loops. No native Programs, Processes, or Opportunities surface.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast is the layer above: it decides what the company should do next, then routes work — including to these agents where appropriate. We compose with them, not against them on code generation.

#### Competitor 2 — Point solutions (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog)

- **Strengths.** Deep ownership of one pipeline each (issues, docs, support, analytics). Loved by users within their domain.
- **Weaknesses.** Each owns one pipeline. The work between them falls on the founder. None coordinate across product, GTM, support, and ops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast doesn't replace them. It observes them, remembers across them, and acts through them — with citations back to the source events.

#### Competitor 3 — Status quo (founder + tabs + context switching)

- **Strengths.** Zero vendor cost. Fully flexible. Founder knows everything in their head.
- **Weaknesses.** Doesn't scale past a handful of people. Context is lost every time the founder steps away. Follow-up drops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Encode the loops the founder already runs in their head as Programs, so the team compounds leverage instead of rebuilding context each week.

_Optional fourth row — agent infrastructure (LangChain, Mem0, Glean). Call out if needed: these are platform components, not startup operating systems._

### Pricing strategy

Per-user pricing at $50 / user / month on the Team tier. Free Solo tier removes the adoption barrier and lets individual founders adopt before paying. Team tier scales linearly with headcount — the more the company grows, the more Lightfast compounds across the org. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra) with custom pricing. Rationale: per-user aligns revenue with actual usage density and matches the mental model of every comparable developer tool (Linear, Notion, Intercom).

### SWOT analysis

**Strengths.**
- Clear positioning — "OS for startups" is a category the market is starting to name but no one owns yet.
- Working runtime already in production (see SPEC.md `v0.1.0` entities).
- Founder-built with no external funding for 12 months — strong capital efficiency signal.
- 15+ engineering-lead interviews validated the core problem.

**Weaknesses.**
- Team of one. Execution bandwidth is finite.
- Pre-revenue. No live pricing data yet.
- Broad scope (product, GTM, support, ops) risks being seen as "doing everything" before any surface is deeply loved.
- Category ambiguity — "operating system" is a claim that requires proof.

**Opportunities.**
- Coding agents are hitting mainstream adoption (75% of Linear enterprise workspaces run coding agents; agents authored ~25% of new issues last quarter). The coordination layer is the next bottleneck.
- Modern APIs now reach inbox, repo, CRM, and error log — the integration surface is finally addressable.
- Every YC batch adds thousands of small teams to the target market annually.
- Answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity) create a new inbound channel that rewards structured, citable content.

**Threats.**
- Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) expand scope into company operations.
- A well-funded competitor lands the "startup OS" category first.
- Platform risk — core connected tools (GitHub, Linear) restrict integration surface or build similar features natively.
- Macro tightening slows seed fundraising and small-team formation.

#### How we'll address each weakness and threat

- **Team of one / bandwidth** — close seed round, make first 1–2 senior hires, lean heavily on Lightfast itself to compound leverage.
- **Pre-revenue / category ambiguity** — ship Team tier to paid design partners before broad launch; let one surface (Opportunities or Memory) carry the initial wedge story.
- **Horizontal agents expanding** — compose with them, not against them; own the Programs / Opportunities layer they don't build.
- **Platform risk** — vendor-abstract every integration (the `@vendor/*` pattern already enforces this) and invest in webhooks-first ingestion that degrades gracefully.
- **Macro** — capital efficiency is already demonstrated (12 months at $0); the seed ask is sized for 12–18 months of runway.

---

## Risk management

### Risk assessment

> Draft — please review likelihoods and add anything I've missed.

| Risk / impact | Likelihood | What we'll do to reduce this risk |
|---|---|---|
| Key person risk — founder incapacitated | Low | Document all systems, automate backups, set up access recovery for all critical accounts. |
| Data loss from customer-tenant data | Low | Tenant isolation, DB backups, disaster-recovery runbook. [TBD — formal runbook.] |
| Security breach / credential leak | Medium | Vendor abstractions with scoped credentials, secret rotation, [TBD — formal security review cadence]. |
| Platform dependency (GitHub / Linear API changes) | **High** | Vendor abstraction layer `@vendor/*`; webhook-first with graceful degradation. Monitor API deprecations proactively. Maintain fallback polling paths. |
| Fundraise fails / runway shortens | Medium | Ship paid Team tier early; capital efficiency demonstrated. |
| Competitor lands "startup OS" category first | **High** | Ship faster on Programs + Opportunities; build AEO distribution moat; lock early design partners with multi-year agreements where sensible. |
| AI provider outage / pricing shock (Anthropic, OpenAI) | **High** | Multi-provider abstraction; degrade to cached results where possible. Hedge spend across providers. Watch token-pricing trends and build in price-pass-through on Team + Scale tiers. |
| Regulatory change (AU privacy, EU AI Act) | Low | Privacy-by-default architecture already in place (no training on customer data). |

### Insurance

Nothing currently in place. Required before paid customers onboard.

| Insurance type | Status | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Professional Indemnity | Not in place | Required before Team tier paid launch — covers claims arising from professional services. |
| Cyber insurance | Not in place | Required before Team tier paid launch — covers data breach / credential leak incidents. Strongly recommended given customer data custody. |
| Public Liability | Not in place | Standard Australian business cover — lower priority for pure SaaS with no physical premises visitors. |
| Business interruption | Not in place | Review once revenue is material. |
| Workers compensation | Not applicable yet | Required by law once first employee is hired in Victoria. |

### Succession plan

Solo-founder stage. The founder is the sole operator and sole director, which concentrates key-person risk. Mitigations:

- **Credentials and access recovery.** All critical accounts (domain registrar, cloud providers, DNS, code repositories, payments, banking) use the founder's primary identity with a documented recovery procedure. A sealed credential-recovery kit is kept with the lawyer (Luna Startup Lawyers) to be opened only on incapacity.
- **Staged advisor handover.** In the event the founder is incapacitated, Luna Startup Lawyers acts as first contact to notify investors, the advisor, and any paying customers, and initiates a wind-down or sale process consistent with SAFE terms and fiduciary obligations.
- **Key-person insurance.** Life / TPD insurance on the founder to be taken out once revenue is material enough to warrant the premium (target: post-Series A, or earlier if investors request it as a condition of the SAFE).
- **First senior hire.** Once the first senior engineer is onboarded post-seed, a continuity plan is documented so they can keep the runtime operational for at least 90 days without founder involvement.
- **Voluntary step-down.** If the founder chooses to step down, preference is given to the next senior operator (likely the first senior engineer hire) with a standard vesting-acceleration and share-buyback process governed by the shareholder agreement.

### Laws we'll need to comply with

- Australian Privacy Act 1988 (customer data handling).
- Spam Act 2003 (marketing communications).
- Consumer law (Australian Consumer Law under Competition and Consumer Act 2010).
- GDPR (any EU-resident users).
- Fair Work Act (once first employee is hired).
- [TBD — industry-specific laws if selling into regulated verticals, e.g. healthcare, finance.]

---

| Close Series A | Demonstrate net retention > 120%, gross margin > 75%, repeatable sales motion | [TBD] | Jeevan + board |
| Team of ~8 people across engineering, GTM, support | Seed eng hires (×2) in place · add first GTM / DevRel hire · add support / ops hire · add 2-3 more engineers post-Series A | [TBD] | Jeevan |

We are raising a **$500K SAFE** to fund 12–18 months of runway.

input.old_string:
#### How we'll address each weakness and threat

- **Team of one / bandwidth** — close seed round, make first 1–2 senior hires, lean heavily on Lightfast itself to compound leverage.
- **Pre-revenue / category ambiguity** — ship Team tier to paid design partners before broad launch; let one surface (Opportunities or Memory) carry the initial wedge story.
- **Horizontal agents expanding** — compose with them, not against them; own the Programs / Opportunities layer they don't build.
- **Platform risk** — vendor-abstract every integration (the `@vendor/*` pattern already enforces this) and invest in webhooks-first ingestion that degrades gracefully.
- **Macro** — capital efficiency is already demonstrated (12 months at $0); the seed ask is sized for 12–18 months of runway.

input.old_string:
#### Competitor 1 — Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex)

- **Strengths.** Powerful general agents. Deep Anthropic / OpenAI distribution. Flatten the cost of writing software.
- **Weaknesses.** General-purpose. Execute tasks; do not run a company's operating loops. No native Programs, Processes, or Opportunities surface.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast is the layer above: it decides what the company should do next, then routes work — including to these agents where appropriate. We compose with them, not against them on code generation.

#### Competitor 2 — Point solutions (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog)

- **Strengths.** Deep ownership of one pipeline each (issues, docs, support, analytics). Loved by users within their domain.
- **Weaknesses.** Each owns one pipeline. The work between them falls on the founder. None coordinate across product, GTM, support, and ops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Lightfast doesn't replace them. It observes them, remembers across them, and acts through them — with citations back to the source events.

#### Competitor 3 — Status quo (founder + tabs + context switching)

- **Strengths.** Zero vendor cost. Fully flexible. Founder knows everything in their head.
- **Weaknesses.** Doesn't scale past a handful of people. Context is lost every time the founder steps away. Follow-up drops.
- **What we'll do differently.** Encode the loops the founder already runs in their head as Programs, so the team compounds leverage instead of rebuilding context each week.

input.old_string:
### Pricing strategy

Per-user pricing at $50 / user / month on the Team tier. Free Solo tier removes the adoption barrier and lets individual founders adopt before paying. Team tier scales linearly with headcount — the more the company grows, the more Lightfast compounds across the org. Scale tier handles enterprise requirements (SSO, audit log, dedicated infra) with custom pricing. Rationale: per-user aligns revenue with actual usage density and matches the mental model of every comparable developer tool (Linear, Notion, Intercom).

### SWOT analysis
**Strengths.**

input.old_string:
### Our why

Founders spend their day stitching context between tools. Code is cheaper than ever to write, but the work that surrounds it — deciding what to build next, triaging feedback, closing the loop on support, running GTM, catching errors — is still manual. Lightfast exists because coordination is the next bottleneck, and no one is building the layer that runs it.

### Our vision

Every startup runs on Lightfast. The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the company moving — across product, go-to-market, support, and ops — with the same compounding leverage that coding agents gave engineers.

input.old_string:
**What our business does.** Lightfast is an operating system for startups. It runs the work between tools — Programs, Processes, Memory, and Opportunities — so small teams can ship product, launch, support customers, and close the loop without stitching context by hand.

**Our target market.** Teams of 1 to 30 building software today. Solo founders, seed-stage teams, and Series A teams shipping daily on a fragmented tool stack.

**Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them and routes the work.

Runtime is Next.js (apps), tRPC + Inngest (APIs + workflows), Postgres via Drizzle, Upstash Redis, and vendor-abstracted integrations with GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, and similar tools. Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company. Coding agents flattened the cost of shipping. What remains is everything around code — priorities, support, launches, bugs, follow-up — and it still lives across disconnected tools. Founders and small teams spend their time stitching context together and keeping work from slipping. The product is faster to build. The company is still manual.

- Ship v1 runtime — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities — to design-partner teams.
- Reach $25K MRR and seed-worthy metrics within 6-18 months to unlock a $2M seed round.
- Close $2M seed to fund an 8-person team (5 eng, 1 design, 1 sales, 1 PM).

lines: **Our unique selling point.** Horizontal agents (Claude Code, Codex) execute tasks. Point tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog) each own one pipeline. Lightfast decides what the company should do next across all of them, then routes the work.
**Our business goals.**
-- Close $500K SAFE seed round for 12–18 months of runway.
+- Close $500K pre-seed SAFE to fund the bridge to seed.
- Ship v1 runtime — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities — to design-partner teams.
-- Reach $25K MRR by end of Year 1.
+- Reach $25K MRR and seed-worthy metrics within 6-18 months to unlock a $2M seed round.
+- Close $2M seed to fund an 8-person team (5 eng, 1 design, 1 sales, 1 PM).

**Our history.** 12 months of build. 3,930 commits. $0 external funding to date. Entity incorporated 11 September 2025 (Lightfast Pty Ltd, VIC). Prior experience across AI infrastructure, computational design, and fabrication systems. 15+ engineering-lead interviews surfaced context loss as a recurring top pain point.

content.text: Now the Finance section — restructure as pre-seed + seed phases:

input.old_string:
### Finance needed

We are raising a **$500K SAFE** to fund 12–18 months of runway.

Use of funds (approximate split):

| Category | % of raise | Amount | Covers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering | 60% | $300K | Two senior engineer hires + fractional design contractor over the runway. |
| Cloud infrastructure | 20% | $100K | Vercel, database, Upstash Redis, Inngest, Anthropic / OpenAI token spend. |
| Distribution | 10% | $50K | AEO content production, founder-led outbound, partner listings, community. |
| Legal + ops | 10% | $50K | Trade mark registration, insurance (PI + cyber), contracts, accounting setup, operating runway. |
input.new_string:
### Finance needed

Staged raise across two rounds.

**Round 1 — Pre-seed SAFE, $500K (raising now).**

Funds a 6-18 month bridge to seed-worthy metrics ($25K+ MRR, design-partner retention, repeatable acquisition).

| Category | % of raise | Amount | Covers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering | 55% | $275K | First senior engineer hire + fractional design contractor through the bridge. |
| Cloud infrastructure | 20% | $100K | Vercel, database, Upstash Redis, Inngest, Anthropic / OpenAI token spend. |
| Distribution | 10% | $50K | AEO content production, founder-led outbound, partner listings, community. |
| Legal + ops | 10% | $50K | Trade mark registration, insurance (PI + cyber), contracts, accounting setup. |
| Founder runway | 5% | $25K | Modest founder draw through the bridge. |

**Round 2 — Seed, $2M (targeted 6-18 months from pre-seed close).**

Funds the full 8-person team (5 engineers, 1 designer, 1 sales, 1 PM) for 18-24 months to reach $1M ARR and prepare the Series A.

| Category | % of raise | Amount | Covers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering payroll | 45% | $900K | 5 senior engineers loaded over 18-24 months. |
| Product + design + PM payroll | 15% | $300K | 1 designer + 1 PM loaded. |
| Sales payroll + tooling | 10% | $200K | 1 sales hire (base + on-target commission) + CRM / outreach stack. |
| Cloud infrastructure | 12% | $240K | Step-up infra + AI token spend as user base and pipelines scale. |
| Distribution + GTM | 10% | $200K | AEO content at scale, events, partner marketplace listings, founder + sales outbound. |
| Legal + ops + insurance | 5% | $100K | PI + cyber insurance live, trade-mark extensions (US/EU), contracts, accounting retainer. |
| Reserve / founder runway | 3% | $60K | Contingency + founder compensation step-up. |

String: > **All figures below are rough directional scaffolding** derived from the $500K SAFE, $50/user/month pricing, $25K MRR Y1 target, and $1M ARR Y3 target. Accountant to finalize. AUD.

### Balance forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total assets (cash + receivables + equipment) | ~$250K | ~$400K | ~$1.2M |
| Total liabilities (SAFE → equity on Series A, accounts payable) | ~$520K | ~$540K | ~$50K |
| Net assets | ~-$270K | ~-$140K | ~$1.15M |

_SAFE appears as liability until Series A triggers conversion. Post-conversion in Y3, balance sheet strengthens materially._

### Profit and loss forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|

input.new_string:
> **All figures below are rough directional scaffolding** derived from the $500K pre-seed + $2M seed staging, $50/user/month pricing, $25K MRR Y1 target, and $1M ARR Y3 target. Accountant to finalize. AUD.
>
> Key modelling assumption: pre-seed closes early Y1; seed closes mid-to-late Y1 (6-18 months after pre-seed, trending earlier end of range if $25K MRR is hit). Full 8-person team onboards across Y2.

### Balance forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total assets (cash + receivables + equipment) | ~$1.8M | ~$900K | ~$1.3M |
| Total liabilities (SAFE on pre-seed; seed priced or SAFE; accounts payable) | ~$540K | ~$2.55M | ~$150K |
| Net assets | ~$1.26M | ~-$1.65M | ~$1.15M |

_SAFE appears as liability until conversion (typically at seed priced round or Series A). Post-conversion in Y3, balance sheet strengthens materially._

### Profit and loss forecast

| Item | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross revenue / net sales | ~$120K (ramp $0 → $25K MRR) | ~$600K (ramp $25K → $70K MRR) | ~$1.1M (reach $1M+ ARR run-rate) |
| Cost of revenue (infra + AI tokens) | ~$60K | ~$160K | ~$240K |
| Gross profit | ~$60K | ~$440K | ~$860K |
| Operating expenses (payroll + legal + distribution) | ~$380K | ~$1.6M | ~$1.9M |
| Net profit / loss | ~-$320K | ~-$1.16M | ~-$1.04M (investable loss; Series A funds growth) |

_Y1 burn is modest (2 hires max). Y2 burn steps up sharply as the 8-person team onboards on seed close. Y3 continues investing ahead of Series A. Gross margin improves from ~50% to ~80% as infra amortizes across a larger user base._

### Cash flow forecast (Year 1)

Assumes $500K pre-seed closes early Y1, first senior engineer + design contractor onboard by month 3, seed round closes late Y1 (modelled in November). Monthly burn ~$30K average pre-seed bridge. AUD.

| | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun | Jul | Aug | Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $0 | $2K | $4K | $7K | $10K | $13K | $16K | $18K | $20K | $22K | $24K | $25K |
| Operating costs | $15K | $18K | $30K | $32K | $34K | $34K | $34K | $34K | $34K | $34K | $36K | $60K |
| Fundraise inflow | $500K (pre-seed) | | | | | | | | | | $2.0M (seed) | |
| Monthly cash delta | +$485K | -$16K | -$26K | -$25K | -$24K | -$21K | -$18K | -$16K | -$14K | -$12K | +$1.99M | -$35K |
| Closing balance | $485K | $469K | $443K | $418K | $394K | $373K | $355K | $339K | $325K | $313K | $2.30M | $2.27M |

_Directional only. Accountant to refine once hiring timing, AI token spend, and distribution budget are confirmed. Seed close timing has the biggest impact on Y1 balance — if it slips to Y2, Y1 closing balance lands ~$280K and bridge extends into early Y2._

Done. Draft 3 is live.

**Major restructure this round:**
- **Staged fundraise.** Split into two rounds — $500K pre-seed SAFE (raising now, ~6-18 month bridge) → $2M seed (post-validation, funds the 8-person team)
- **8-person team.** 5 engineers, 1 designer, 1 sales, 1 PM (onboards across Y2 on seed close)
- **Pre-seed hires.** 1 senior engineer + design contractor only — the bridge isn't meant to build the full team
- **Use of funds.** Two separate tables, one per round
- **Balance/P&L/cash flow.** Reworked to show pre-seed inflow in Jan + seed inflow in Nov, Y1 closes at ~$2.27M, Y2 burns ~$1.16M as team scales, Y3 prepares Series A

**Added:**
- Startmate Launch Club current membership in History, Strengths, Advisors table, and Sources of funding
- Blackbird Foundry / Startmate Accelerator application as a Y1 goal (within 6 months, post idea validation)
- Accelerator cheque ($75-120K) as a funding source

**Removed (per your note):**
- "3,930 commits" and "$0 external funding" / "12 months at $0" from all four places they appeared (history, key people, SWOT, mitigations, sources of funding). Replaced with more durable framing that doesn't lean on the scrappy-solo narrative.

message.content: how are we getting 1.8M total balance for Year 1?

content.text:
Good catch — the $1.8M is a mistake. It doesn't reconcile with the cash-flow table, which shows Y1 closing at **$2.27M cash**. Let me walk through the math and fix it.

**Y1 cash inflows:** $500K pre-seed + $2.0M seed + ~$120K revenue = $2.62M
**Y1 cash outflows:** ~$350K operating + ~$60K COGS ≈ $410K total (low because only 1-2 hires land in Y1)
**Y1 closing cash:** ~$2.27M (matches the cash-flow table)
**Plus receivables + equipment:** ~$30K
**Total assets Y1:** **~$2.30M** — not $1.8M.

The $1.8M appeared because I was inconsistently netting SAFE liability against assets in my head. Fixing it now.

lastPrompt: how are we getting 1.8M total balance for Year 1?

content.text:
**Fixed. Balance forecast now reconciles with the cash-flow table.**

The corrected math chain:

| | Y1 | Y2 | Y3 (pre-Series A) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting cash | $0 | $2.27M | $1.11M |
| + Pre-seed SAFE | $500K | — | — |
| + Seed SAFE | $2.0M | — | — |
| + Revenue | $120K | $600K | $1.1M |
| − Total costs | $410K | $1.76M | $2.14M |
| **Ending cash** | **$2.27M** | **$1.11M** | **$0.07M** |

The old table was trying to net SAFE liabilities against assets inconsistently. New version keeps them separate:
- **Total assets Y1 = $2.30M** (cash $2.27M + receivables/equipment $30K)
- **Total liabilities Y1 = $2.52M** (both SAFEs outstanding + ~$20K payables)
- **Net assets Y1 = ~-$220K** (book value is negative because SAFEs haven't converted yet; real shareholder value only visible post-Series A conversion)

Also added a footnote: Series A proceeds aren't modelled in the table (not yet committed), but illustratively a $5M Series A closing mid-Y3 would flip everything positive — cash goes from $0.07M → ~$5M by Y3 end, SAFEs convert to equity, liabilities drop to payables only.

Does the Y3 near-zero cash position look right to you, or should we model an earlier/smaller Series A to close the gap? $500K pre-seed + $2M seed funds roughly 3 years of burn at these assumptions — tight but standard. Slippage on Series A timing is the biggest risk in the forecast.

### Year 3 outlook (CY 2028, pre-Series A)

Y3 is the first full year of the 8-person team at steady state. Monthly all-in cost stabilises around $140K (comp adjustments + infra growth). Revenue ramps $70K → $90K+ MRR to reach $1M+ ARR run-rate by year end. Net burn ~$720K.

Done. All three forecasts (P&L, balance, cash flow) now reconcile with the staged Y1 pre-seed / Y2 seed timing.

**New chain of numbers:**

| | Y1 (2026) | Y2 (2027) | Y3 (2028) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting cash | $0 | $291K | $1.61M |
| + Fundraise | $500K pre-seed | $2.0M seed | — |
| + Revenue | $75K | $570K | $1.02M |
| − Total costs | $290K | $1.25M | $1.73M |
| **Ending cash** | **$291K** | **$1.61M** | **$890K** |
| Net loss (P&L) | -$215K | -$675K | -$710K |

**Runway signal:**
- Y1 bridge: $500K pre-seed → ~$291K at year end (reasonable buffer to absorb seed-close slippage into Q1-Q2 2027)
- Y2 ramp: $2M seed + $291K bridge = $2.29M into the year; full 8-person team in by Q3; closes Y2 at ~$1.6M
- Y3 steady state: $890K cash at Y3 end. Series A process must kick off **mid-Y3 (H1 2028)** to land funds before runway tightens. ~6 months of runway cushion if Series A slips.

**Biggest sensitivity:** if the seed slips a full quarter (to Q2 2027 instead of Jan), Y2 closing balance drops by ~$350K and Y3 runway runs tight by Q3 2028. Worth holding in mind when pacing the seed-raise process.

Want me to model an alternative with a larger seed (e.g., $2.5M or $3M) to de-risk the Y3 gap, or keep at $2M?

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - User (Jeevan Pillay, founder of Lightfast Pty Ltd, Melbourne) asked to copy `/Users/jeevanpillay/Downloads/Business plan template doc (1).docx` (Australian business.gov.au template) into the Lightfast repo and fill it out based on Lightfast's description (SPEC.md).
   - Agreed to use markdown mirror approach at `thoughts/shared/business-plan.md` rather than editing .docx directly (no pandoc available).
   - Multiple iterative rounds of refinement using AskUserQuestion for decisions.
   - Final substantive restructures: (a) editorial pass to match Linear-cadence voice rules, (b) team expansion to 8 hires, (c) fundraise split into $500K pre-seed → $2M seed, (d) shift seed closing from late Y1 to early Y2 (2027).
   - User wants business plan aligned with Lightfast's existing positioning as "operating system for startups" (not "memory layer" — stale framing).

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Australian business plan template (business.gov.au) with Pty Ltd/ABN/ACN/GST fields
   - Lightfast: operating system for 1-30 person startup teams — Programs, Processes, Memory, Opportunities primitives
   - Staged fundraise: $500K pre-seed SAFE (May 2026) → $2M seed SAFE (Jan 2027) → Series A (mid-late 2028)
   - 8-person team composition: 5 engineers + 1 designer + 1 sales + 1 product manager
   - Voice rules from pitch deck: Linear cadence, short declaratives, active verbs (run, route, orchestrate, compose, compound, carry, ship), no hype words (empower, unleash, transform), capitalize proprietary nouns, numbers > adjectives
   - Startmate Launch Club membership; Blackbird Foundry / Startmate Accelerator as Y1 goal
   - SAFEs as liabilities until Series A conversion
   - Linear-cadence formatting per `thoughts/shared/pitch-deck-2026-04.md`

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/thoughts/shared/business-plan.md` (CREATED)
      - Full markdown mirror of the .docx template with all sections populated
      - Most recent edits focus on staged Y1/Y2/Y3 financial tables that reconcile
      - Key sections: Status checklist, Key details (registration/contact/online), The business (summary/why/vision/mission/operations/key people), The market (problem/solution/target market/advertising/competition/pricing/SWOT), Risk management (risks/insurance/succession/laws), Goals, Finances (staged use of funds, balance/P&L/cash flow Y1+Y2, Y3 narrative)
   - `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/SPEC.md` (READ for Lightfast mission/vision/problem/solution)
   - `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/thoughts/shared/pitch-deck-2026-04.md` (READ for voice rules, positioning, pricing, fundraise targets, founder bio)
   - `/Users/jeevanpillay/.claude/projects/-Users-jeevanpillay-Code--lightfastai-lightfast/memory/project_lightfast_positioning_os.md` (READ for current "startup OS" thesis)
   - `/tmp/business-plan-template.txt` (extracted text of the .docx via `textutil`)

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Y1 balance forecast inconsistency**: I had $1.8M total assets for Y1 while cash flow showed $2.27M. User caught it: "how are we getting 1.8M total balance for Year 1?" Fixed by properly separating assets from liabilities (assets ~$2.30M, liabilities $2.52M with both SAFEs outstanding, net -$220K). Later superseded by Y2 seed move.
   - **Incorrect Y1 revenue in P&L**: After moving seed to Y2, had to recompute — revenue ramp $0 → $25K MRR by December yields ~$75K total Y1 revenue, not $120K or $160K as in earlier drafts.
   - **User feedback — commits/funding claims**: "i dont need we need 4k commits and 0 external funding." I stripped "3,930 commits" and "$0 external funding" from 5 places (history, key people table, SWOT strengths, macro mitigation, sources of funding). Replaced with more durable framing.
   - **User feedback — team size**: Proposed 8-person team but pointed out $500K SAFE couldn't fund it; user responded with "we intend to do pre-seed $500k -> $2M seed raise in 6-18 months" — led to two-round staging restructure.
   - **User feedback — timing**: User pointed out we're already in April Y1 and asked to move seed to Y2 (next year, 2027) rather than late Y1.
   - **Edit failures**: Two cases where Edit tool failed because content drifted — recovered by Grep/Read for exact strings.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Solved: Mapped a comprehensive Australian SaaS business plan from scratch using pre-existing positioning documents (SPEC.md, pitch deck, memory).
   - Solved: Three-round iterative fact gathering via AskUserQuestion minimized back-and-forth.
   - Solved: Staged two-round fundraise model (pre-seed + seed) with reconciled balance/P&L/cash flow across three years.
   - Solved: Y1=2026 / Y2=2027 / Y3=2028 calendar-year timeline with pre-seed May 2026 + seed Jan 2027.
   - Ongoing: Final numbers await accountant review; Series A amount not yet committed (illustrated as $5M); full registered address is complete.

6. All user messages:
   - "have a look at this docx file: /Users/jeevanpillay/Downloads/Business plan template doc (1).docx / we need to start filling this up based on Lightfast's description. / can we copy over the business plan to this repo and start working on this docx"
   - "makes sense go with markdown approach."
   - "perfect. yes proceed. using AskUserQuestions when needed"
   - Answer batch 1: Pty Ltd / $500K SAFE not yet closed / per-user $50/month / Home office Melbourne
   - Answer batch 2: Luna Startup Lawyers (wants to engage for accounts/bookkeeping too) / No insurance yet / Trade mark not yet filed / $25K MRR
   - Answer batch 3: 2 senior engineers + design contractor / 1-3 unpaid design partners / 60/20/10/10 use of funds / $1M ARR Y3
   - Answer batch 4: Remove technical advisor row / Standard solo-founder succession / HIGH risks on competitor+platform+AI provider / Founder laptop only
   - "Lightfast Pty Ltd / ABN: 35 690 889 984 / ACN: 690 889 984 / Date of Registration: 11 Sep 2025 / Phone: +61433970179 / LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/lightfastai / X: https://x.com/lightfastai / GitHub: http://github.com/lightfastai/ / No laptop yet."
   - "1. 51 Grosvenor St, South Yarra, 3141, VIC / 2. Accountant we havent done yet. / and yes to editorial pass"
   - "i think we need to expand the team more. 5 engineers. 1 designer. 1 sales. 1 product manager."
   - "[Request interrupted by user] we intend to do pre-seed $500k -> $2M seed raise in 6-18 months."

# Lightfast Pitch Deck — Working Draft (2026-04)

> Working content for the seed pitch deck being designed in Paper ("Daring ember" file).
> Format: 10 slides, 1440×900, dark editorial system, single red moment on slide 1.
> Voice: Linear cadence — short declaratives, active mechanical verbs, no hype words.
> Recurring nouns: Company → Program · Validation · Growth · Scale

---

## Slide 01 — Title `[LOCKED]`

- **H1**: The operating system for startups.
- **Sub**: Accelerating the path from validation to scale.
- **Chrome**: `LIGHTFAST` · `PITCH · APRIL 2026` · `SEED ROUND` · `01 / 10 — TITLE`

---

## Slide 02 — Problem

- **H1**: Building software is no longer the hard part of building a company.
- **Kicker** (mono, smaller): Code got cheaper. Running the company didn't.
- **Body** (3 short paragraphs):
  1. Small teams can ship product faster than ever.
  2. Priorities, support, launches, bugs, and follow-up still live across disconnected tools.
  3. Founders spend their time stitching context together and keeping work from slipping.
- **Closing line** (large, anchors the slide): The product is faster to build. The company is still manual.

_Open: do we name specific tools (Linear, Notion, Intercom, Slack) or stay abstract?_

---

## Slide 03 — Solution

- **H1**: Lightfast gives startups one system to run the company.
- **Sub**: One system across product, go-to-market, support, and ops.
- **Body** — three OS primitives, each a one-liner block:
  - **Processes** — Every pipeline runs in one system with clear state.
  - **Memory** — Customer context, decisions, and follow-up stay attached to the work.
  - **Opportunities** — The system surfaces what the team could do next across product, growth, support, and ops.
- **Closing line**: The founder sets the direction. Lightfast keeps the work moving.

_Open: should "Programs" be capitalised everywhere as a proper noun? (My vote: yes — it's the proprietary noun, like Linear's "Issues" or Stripe's "Charges".)_

---

## Slide 04 — Why Now

- **H1**: Code is commoditized. The company isn't — yet.
- **Sub**: The systems are finally addressable. The operating layer is still missing.
- **Body** (4 short claims):
  1. Coding agents are now installed in 75% of Linear's enterprise workspaces and authored 25% of new issues last quarter.
  2. Modern APIs and agent tooling can now reach the inbox, repo, CRM, and error log.
  3. The bottleneck moved up a layer: how the company runs.
  4. The opening is the system that coordinates work across all of it.
- **Closing line** (large, anchors the slide): The next bottleneck is coordination.

---

## Slide 05 — Market

- **H1**: Every team of 1 to 30. Every founder shipping today.
- **Sub**: The biggest cohort in software, and the most underserved.
- **Body** — three columns:
  - **WHO**
    - Solo founders
    - 2–10 person seed teams
    - 10–30 person Series A teams
  - **HOW MANY**
    - 80K+ apply to YC every year
    - ~50K seed-stage companies funded annually (US)
    - Millions of solo builders globally
  - **WHY UNDERSERVED**
    - Most tooling is built for one function at a time
    - Small teams carry the overlap themselves
- **Sizing block** (mono, end of slide):
  - SOM `TBD` — update once pricing and seat model are locked
  - SAM `TBD` — draft 1 placeholder
  - TAM Every team running a startup on a fragmented tool stack

---

## Slide 06 — Competitive Landscape

- **H1**: Horizontal agents can do work. Lightfast runs the company.
- **Sub**: Claude Code and Codex are the biggest platform risk. They are broad agents, not startup operating systems.
- **Body** — three categories, each a column or row:
  - **HORIZONTAL AGENTS** — Claude Code, Codex. Powerful general agents for building software and automating tasks across toolchains.
  - **APPS (point solutions)** — Linear, Notion, Intercom, PostHog. Each owns one pipeline.
  - **STATUS QUO** — The founder. Their browser tabs. Their context-switching. Their burnout.
- **Anchor line** (large, end of slide): General agents execute tasks. Lightfast decides what the company should do next.

_Open: do we want a tiny fourth note for "agent infrastructure" (LangChain, Mem0, Glean), or is that unnecessary for draft 1?_

---

## Slide 07 — Operating Landscape

- **H1**: Startups run the same loops every week.
- **Sub**: Build, launch, support, learn, and follow up in one place.
- **Body** — simple grid or columns, no center diagram:
  - **BUILD** — ship features and fixes
  - **LAUNCH** — publish, announce, distribute
  - **SUPPORT** — close tickets and unblock users
  - **FEEDBACK** — turn conversations into priorities
  - **OPS** — keep work moving
  - **ERRORS** — catch, triage, and fix what breaks
- **Closing line**: One system for the work between tools.

_Alt if this still feels too broad: make the slide about fragmentation instead — "The work doesn't live in one tool." Then show product, support, docs, chat, and founder follow-up as the gap Lightfast covers._

---

## Slide 08 — Product

- **H1**: How the OS works.
- **Sub**: Four primitives. Built once. Composable forever.
- **Body** — four blocks (2×2 grid or stacked):
  - **PROGRAMS** — Plain-language operating spec for the company.
  - **PROCESSES** — Managed workflows with clear state.
  - **MEMORY** — Shared context across the work.
  - **OPPORTUNITIES** — Surfaces the highest-leverage things a user could do next across product, customer acquisition, support, and ops.
- **Closing line**: The company runs on shared context instead of constant follow-up.

---

## Slide 09 — Business Model

- **H1**: Pay per team. Every plan unlocks the runtime.
- **Sub**: Free for solo founders. Priced for the next 30 hires.
- **Body** — three pricing tiers:
  - **SOLO — Free**
    - 1 user
    - 3 pipelines
    - Core memory
  - **TEAM — $50 / month**
    - Up to 30 users
    - All seven pipelines
    - Agent + MCP access
  - **SCALE — Custom**
    - 30+ users
    - SSO, audit log, dedicated infra
- **Margin line** (mono, end): High-margin software model with usage costs to manage.

_Open: confirm pricing. Old data said $20/user/mo — is that current? "Per team" or "per user"? I have it as per-user but per-team feels stronger for the OS framing (one license per company)._

---

## Slide 10 — Founder

- **H1**: Jeevan Pillay
- **Sub**: Founder & CEO · Melbourne, Australia
- **Tagline**: Built across AI infrastructure, computational design, and fabrication systems.
- **Body** — three blocks (matches Linear's "compress everything into three columns" pattern):
  - **BUILDING**
    - **12 months. 3,930 commits. $0 external funding.**
    - Built ingestion, retrieval, and orchestration systems across the stack.
  - **SYSTEMS**
    - Computational design, generative systems, and code-to-fabrication workflows.
    - Led technical campaigns generating $1M+ in revenue.
  - **VALIDATION**
    - 15+ engineering lead interviews.
    - Context loss came up repeatedly as a top pain point.
- **Closing chrome**: `jp@lightfast.ai` · `RAISING $500K SAFE` · `12-18 MONTH RUNWAY`

_Open: confirm the ask number, runway, and contact line._

---

## Open questions to resolve before we commit copy

1. **Slide 9** — confirm pricing structure around the new `$50 / month` direction.
2. **Slide 10** — confirm $500K SAFE, 12–18 month runway, and contact email.

---

## Voice rules (apply consistently across every slide)

- **Verbs to use**: run, schedule, route, orchestrate, compose, compound, absorb, carry, ground, collapse, ship.
- **Verbs to avoid**: empower, unleash, transform, revolutionize, supercharge.
- **Sentence shape**: short declaratives. Punchline first, explanation after.
- **No hype words**: no "AI-powered", no "revolutionary", no exclamations, no emojis.
- **No fuzzy metaphors**: avoid lines like "founder as the integration layer," "human glue," "ambient intelligence," or "self-driving startup" unless they can be defended in literal operating terms.
- **Capitalize proprietary nouns**: Program, Pipeline, Memory, Opportunity. Like Linear capitalizes Issues.
- **Numbers > adjectives**: "12 months, 3,930 commits" > "deep experience".

name: Lightfast positioning — operating system for startups
description: Lightfast's current product thesis (Apr 2026): an OS that runs the startup-building lifecycle for 1-30 person teams, not a memory layer
type: project
originSessionId: 0091b36e-3d7e-49e4-bbf3-e9906334732b
---
Lightfast is positioned as an **operating system that runs the entire startup-building lifecycle** for 1–30 person teams. It orchestrates pipelines across ops, GTM, dev, design, support, feedback, and errors. Unifies company knowledge into expressible logic reusable anywhere. Ambient intelligence puts startup-building on autopilot.

Why-now thesis: **code is commoditized** (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex have flattened the cost of shipping). The remaining hard problem is everything *around* code — knowing what to build next, customer feedback triage, GTM motions, support, ops. If code is solved, the new bottleneck is the orchestration of the company itself. Lightfast commoditizes building a startup the way Claude Code commoditized writing one.

**Why:** Prior framings — "memory layer for teams," "search API with citations," the apps/www/src/config/pitch-deck-data.ts copy — are stale and undersell the scope. Talking about Lightfast as a memory product misrepresents the current vision.

**How to apply:** Frame Lightfast as a startup OS, not a memory layer or search product. The memory layer is one process the OS runs, not the headline. When writing pitch/marketing/docs copy, lead with the OS framing and "ambient intelligence → autopilot" outcome.

# Lightfast Specification

Status: Draft v0 (high-level, language-agnostic)

Last Updated: 2026-04-15

Purpose: Define the operating infrastructure between agents and apps — the layer through which agents and teams observe, reason, and act across a product-development tool stack.

---

## Mission

Be the operating layer for every team — so agents and people can observe, reason, and act across the entire tool stack through a single system.

## Vision

Any agent or engineer can understand what is happening, why it happened, and what should happen next — across every tool, every team, every decision — without knowing which tools exist or how they work.

---

## 1. Problem Statement

Lightfast is an operating layer that sits between agents and apps. The layer ingests events from connected tools, stores them as a cited temporal graph, resolves intent into action against the correct tool, and enforces team-defined invariants continuously.

The layer addresses four operational problems:

- Tool memory is siloed. Events live only in the tool that produced them, so no system correlates causality, ownership, or relationships across the stack.
- Agents target tools directly. Every new agent means N×M integrations, drifting API usage, and no shared context across runs.
- Team rules are enforced by convention. Release checks, review gates, and policy rules live in documents, not in a kernel that applies them at all times.
- Answers cannot cite their source. Summaries without evidence cannot be trusted when humans or agents act on them.

Important boundary:

- Lightfast is an operating layer. It mediates between tools; it does not replace them.
- Lightfast is not a data warehouse, a BI tool, or a general-purpose observability platform.
- A successful operation ends at a cited answer or a resolved action against a connected tool, not at raw data delivery.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

### 2.1 Goals

- Observe every event across every connected tool automatically and continuously.
- Store events as immutable, causally ordered facts with semantic embeddings in a temporal graph.
- Cite every answer, action, and decision back to source events.
- Resolve agent intent into action against the correct connected tool without per-agent integration work.
- Enforce team-defined invariants continuously, not on a schedule.
- Expose the same primitives to agents and to people (REST API, SDK, MCP, webhooks).
- Preserve complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for model training.

### 2.2 Non-Goals

- Replacing existing tools in the stack. Teams keep using what they use.
- General-purpose analytics, business intelligence, or data warehousing.
- Black-box decisions. Every action traces to source events; no summary ships without verification.
- Rebuilding a unified UI for every connected tool.
- Agent orchestration as a product surface. Agents operate through Lightfast; Lightfast does not orchestrate agents.

## 3. System Overview

### 3.1 Abstraction Layers

Lightfast is a pipeline: **Observe → Remember → Reason → Act.** Each layer is a distinct concern.

1. `Observe`
   - Ingests events from connected tools via webhooks, polling, or push APIs.
   - Normalizes platform payloads into a stable event model.

2. `Remember`
   - Stores events as immutable facts with causal ordering.
   - Resolves entities across tools (commits, issues, deployments, incidents, people).
   - Maintains a temporal graph and semantic embeddings over those entities.

3. `Reason`
   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
   - Detects patterns, predicts outcomes, and enforces invariants.
   - Produces cited answers to queries from agents and people.

4. `Act`
   - Resolves intent into write operations against the correct connected tool.
   - Performs side effects (PR comments, issue updates, messages, deploys) with provenance attached.

### 3.2 Main Components

1. `Connection Layer`
   - Manages OAuth flows, credential storage, webhook registration, and backfills per tool.
   - Further specification deferred.

2. `Event Store`
   - Append-only store of normalized events with causal ordering.
   - Further specification deferred.

3. `Graph and Embeddings`
   - Temporal graph of entities and relationships with semantic embeddings.
   - Further specification deferred.

4. `Reasoning Runtime`
   - Executes skills, workflows, and rules against memory.
   - Further specification deferred.

5. `Action Resolver`
   - Translates intent into write operations against connected tools.
   - Further specification deferred.

6. `Access Surfaces`
   - REST API, TypeScript SDK, MCP server, webhooks.
   - Further specification deferred.

### 3.3 External Dependencies

- Connected tools (for example GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel). The set is extensible.
- Identity provider for tenant authentication.
- Vector index and graph storage.
- Background job runtime for ingestion and long-running workflows.

## 4. Core Domain Model

Entity field specifications are deferred to a future revision. This section lists the primary entity types and their present state.

Status tags:
- `(shipped v0.1.0)` — entity exists in the running system and is covered by the public API.
- `(partial v0.1.0)` — entity exists in limited form; the role defined here is broader than what ships.
- `(planned)` — entity is part of the designed system but has no implementation yet.

### 4.1 Entities

#### 4.1.1 Event `(shipped v0.1.0)`

An immutable fact emitted by a connected tool and normalized into the Lightfast event model. Once recorded, an event is never modified.

#### 4.1.2 Entity `(shipped v0.1.0)`

A resolved object in the temporal graph — for example a commit, issue, deployment, incident, or person — synthesized from one or more events.

#### 4.1.3 Connection `(shipped v0.1.0)`

A binding between a tenant and a connected tool, including credentials, scopes, and lifecycle state.

#### 4.1.4 Skill `(planned)`

A composable capability that reads memory and optionally invokes the Action Resolver.

#### 4.1.5 Workflow `(planned)`

An ordered composition of skills, triggered by events or schedules.

#### 4.1.6 Rule `(planned)`

A team-defined invariant continuously enforced against memory.

#### 4.1.7 Agent `(partial v0.1.0)`

A principal — human or automated — that interacts with Lightfast through an access surface. In `v0.1.0` an agent is any caller authenticated by an API key or session token; the expanded role (identity, permissions scoped per skill and rule) is planned.

## 5. Principles

- **Primitives over features.** Every capability is a configuration of the same building blocks, not a new subsystem.
- **Events are facts.** Immutable, causally ordered. Interpretations layer on top. History is never rewritten.
- **Intent over API calls.** Agents express what they want. The system resolves how.
- **Cite everything.** Every answer, every action, every decision traces back to source events.
- **Privacy by default.** Complete tenant isolation. Customer data is never used for training.

## 6. Measures

- **Coverage.** Fraction of a team's tool stack observed and connected.
- **Precision.** Accuracy of cited answers against ground-truth source events.
- **Latency.** Time from event ingestion to availability in memory, and from query to cited answer.
- **Trust.** Fraction of answers and actions with verifiable source evidence and causal rationale.
- **Adoption.** Time-to-integrate per tool and density of agent activity routed through the layer.

---

## Deferred Sections

The following sections are intentionally omitted at this draft level and will be populated in future revisions:

- Detailed field definitions for each entity in Section 4.
- Event lifecycle and state machines.
- Consistency, ordering, and idempotency guarantees.
- Authorization model and scope primitives.
- Error classes and recovery behavior.
- Observability contract (logs, metrics, traces).
- Conformance and compatibility rules.
